The putative companion may contribute to the significant ionization seen in the J1131+6044 PDLA (e.g. high SiIV fraction), despite the large positive velocity offset from the QSO (2424 , but see below for further discussion on the interpretation of velocities).

gmw_00008284.name: PDLA towards J1240+1455
gmw_00008284.overview: The high-redshift galaxy J1240+1455 is a radio-quiet quasar at a redshift of z=3.11 [arXiv:2402.08488]. It is surrounded by a filamentary Lyman-alpha blob extending up to 122 kiloparsecs in diameter and contains a proximate damped Lyman-alpha absorber with high dust depletion [arXiv:1004.2715, arXiv:2402.08488].

sources.summary: The paper describes the J1240+1455 PDLA as a high-column absorber with Lyman-alpha emission, unusually strong NV, high depletion, and dust-driven Si/Fe enhancement.

quotes.quote: J1240+1455, log \ = 21.3 270 Selected metal lines are shown for the PDLA towards J1240+1455 in Figure 1240_metals . This PDLA was studied by Hennawi et al. (2009) using the original SDSS spectrum. Like several other PDLAs (M ller Warren 1993; M ller et al. 1998; Leibundgut Robertson 1999; Ellison et al. 2002) this absorber has Lya emission superimposed in the DLA trough.

J1240+1455 appears to have particularly high depletion, although at Z 1/10 Z_ it is also one of the more metal-rich absorbers. The PDLA towards J1240+1455 has a high \ so the high [ Zn/Fe ] ratio is unlikely to be caused by ionization.

J1240+1455 has an extremely high [ Si/Fe ] that is unlikely to be due to ionization effects, given its high . However, as discussed in the previous subsection, the [ Zn/Fe ] indicates a large depletion fraction which could lead to an over-estimate of [ Si/Fe ] (Fe is much more refractory than Si). Using the undepleted ratio of [ S/Zn ] =0.11, the high [ Si/Fe ] of J1240+1455 appears to be largely due to dust.

sources.summary: The paper studies the radio-quiet quasar SDSS J1240+1455 at z=3.11, using its proximate damped Ly system to image and constrain polarization of the surrounding Ly blob.

quotes.quote: The radio quiet quasar SDSS J1240+1455 lies at a redshift of z=3.11 , is surrounded by a Ly blob (LAB), and is absorbed by a proximate damped Ly system. In order to better define the morphology of the blob and determine its emission mechanism, we gathered deep narrow-band images isolating the Ly line of this object in linearly polarized light. We provide a deep intensity image of the blob, showing a filamentary structure extending up to 16 " (or 122 physical kpc) in diameter. No significant polarization signal could be extracted from the data, but 95 probability upper limits were defined through simulations. They vary between 3 in the central 0.75 \ disk (after subtraction of the unpolarized quasar continuum) and 10 in the 3.8-5.5 " annulus.

gmw_00008285.name: PDLA towards J1604+3951
gmw_00008285.overview: The PDLA towards J1604+3951 is a high-redshift galaxy system [arXiv:1004.2715]. Its low-ionization gas exhibits two main velocity components that show different levels of depletion [arXiv:1004.2715].

sources.summary: The paper reports that the J1604+3951 PDLA has two low-ionization components, broad absorption, similar singly and multiply ionized structure, component-dependent depletion, and saturated CII fine-structure absorption.

quotes.quote: J1604+3951, log \ = 21.75 270 270 The PDLA towards J1604+3951 exhibits two main components in the low ionization gas separated by about 250 , see Figure 1604_low . However, the strongest transitions, such as CII 1334 and AlII 1670 show almost continuous absorption over 600 . Singly and multiply ionized species exhibit quite similar velocity structure, e.g. SiII and SiIV (Figure 1604_high ). In the alpha elements (Si and S) the redder component is stronger, but in Fe-peak (Fe and Ni) the blue component is stronger.
J1604+3951 shows clear signs of different levels of depletion between two velocity components with high [ Zn/Fe ] in one component but not the other (see Section 1604_sec ).

Application to the data CII ^ is detected in Q0151+048 and J1604+3951 and we have upper limits for J0140 0839, J1131+6044 and J2321+1421 which will yield lower distance limits for a given _ . However, the CII ^ towards J1604+3951 is partly blended with CII 1334, and the unblended components are saturated, so we do not consider this PDLA further.

gmw_00008286.name: PDLA towards J2321+1421
gmw_00008286.overview: PDLA towards J2321+1421 is a high-redshift absorber located at a redshift of approximately z = 2.5731 [arXiv:1004.2715]. It exhibits a relatively simple velocity structure with constrained column densities of iron and silicon, but it does not show nitrogen V absorption [arXiv:1004.2715].

sources.summary: The paper describes the J2321+1421 PDLA as a simple absorber centered near z=2.5731, with constrained FeII and SiII columns, no NV, and fine-structure distance limits.

quotes.quote: J2321 + 1421, log \ = 20.70 270 The PDLA towards J2321+1421 has a relatively simple velocity structure with most of the absorption centred within 40 \ of z_ abs = 2.5731 , see Figure 2321_metals . N(FeII) is determined from a simultaneous fit to FeII 1608 and FeII 2374, both of which are unsaturated. SiII 1808 is a maginal detection, whereas SiII 1304 and SiII 1526 are mildly saturated. Nonetheless, combining the 3 transitions we are able to constrain the total SiII column density.
However, J2321+1421 which has the largest negative velocity in our sample ( 1600 ) does not exhibit NV.
Application to the data CII ^ is detected in Q0151+048 and J1604+3951 and we have upper limits for J0140 0839, J1131+6044 and J2321+1421 which will yield lower distance limits for a given _ .

gmw_00008287.name: PDLA towards Q2343 BX415
gmw_00008287.overview: The high-redshift galaxy PDLA towards Q2343 BX415 is characterized by a high metallicity [arXiv:1004.2715]. It features a highly ionized gas component offset by 500 kilometers per second, which may be associated with outflowing material from its host quasar [arXiv:1004.2715].

sources.summary: The paper uses the Q2343 BX415 PDLA as a comparison system with high metallicity, detected NV, offset highly ionized gas, and a possible quasar-host outflow origin.

quotes.quote: Rix et al. (2007) also detected NV in the =20.98 PDLA towards Q2343 BX415, which also has a fairly low velocity separation ( V = 22 ) and high metallicity ( Z 1/5 Z_ ).
Rix et al. (2007) also found a highly ionized component (observed in NV, CIV and SiIV) offset by +500 \ from the PDLA towards Q2343 BX415.
Rix et al. (2007) suggest that the PDLA towards Q2343 BX415 (included in our literature sample) may be associated with outflowing material from the quasar host.

gmw_00008288.name: eRO-QPE3
gmw_00008288.overview: The astronomical source eRO-QPE3 was discovered through a single-flare-search method [arXiv:2403.17059]. It exhibits long bursts, a decaying peak luminosity over time, and a time-varying amplitude with a crudely estimated average recurrence [arXiv:2403.17059].

sources.summary: The paper describes eRO-QPE3 as a single-flare-search discovery with decaying peak luminosity, long bursts and recurrence, time-varying amplitude, and uncertain average recurrence.

quotes.quote: For instance, this is instead the method that found eRO-QPE3 Arcodia+2024:qpe34 .

For eRO-QPE3, since the peak luminosity decayed over time Arcodia+2024:qpe34 , we adopted the average peak luminosity between the eRASS1 to eRASS4 epoch, L_ 0.5-2.0 keV ^ peak =9.7 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 , when the source was actually detected by our algorithm.

An obvious exception is eRO-QPE3 which appears as an outlier in the top panel, surrounded by a region with 0 . This is probably due to the fact that eRO-QPE3 showed time-varying amplitude over the eRASS surveys Arcodia+2024:qpe34 , so our simplistic simulations have not grasped that sources can move across the grid. We also note that the average recurrence is only estimated crudely for eRO-QPE3 Arcodia+2024:qpe34 .

gmw_00008289.name: eRO-QPE4
gmw_00008289.overview: The astronomical object eRO-QPE4 is a source identified through a single-flare search [arXiv:2403.17059]. It exhibits quasi-periodic eruptions with an average peak X-ray luminosity of 4e42 erg/s and a light-curve time shift of 20 hours [arXiv:2403.17059].

sources.summary: The paper identifies eRO-QPE4 as a QPE found by a single-flare search, assigns an average XMM-Newton peak luminosity, and adopts a 20-hour light-curve time shift.

quotes.quote: This is instead the method that found eRO-QPE4 Arcodia+2024:qpe34 .
For eRO-QPE4, we used the average between the three XMM-Newton peaks, namely L_ 0.5-2.0 keV ^ peak =4 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 Arcodia+2024:qpe34 .
This time shift is adopted to be 4.5 d for eRO-QPE1 (given the high scatter observed; Arcodia+2021:eroqpes ; Chakraborty+2024:ero1 ), 5 h for eRO-QPE2, 20.5 h for eRO-QPE3 and 20 h for eRO-QPE4.

gmw_00008290.name: foreground galaxy
gmw_00008290.overview: This foreground galaxy is located at a redshift of z=1.1168 [arXiv:1404.6014]. It acts as a strong gravitational lens for a more distant host galaxy and has a stellar population estimated to be 1 Gyr old [arXiv:1404.6014].

sources.summary: The foreground galaxy is identified as the z=1.1168 lens of PS1-10afx, with a 1 Gyr stellar population, near-zero extinction, and mass/velocity-dispersion properties consistent with strong lensing.

quotes.quote: Given these identifications and the extended nature of the emission (see Fig. fig:lens_OII ), it is clear that the second object is a galaxy lying in front of \ and its host. Indeed, the near maximum light spectra of \ show what could be Ca 2 absorption from this foreground galaxy (Fig. fig:compare_spec ). With its redshift secure, we next checked whether the foreground galaxy can satisfy the lens requirements of . To do this, we derived a stellar mass for the foreground galaxy and used an empirical relation between stellar mass and 1-D velocity dispersion as explained below.

The best-fit SSP combination was a 1 Gyr old foreground galaxy with a stellar mass of (9 2) 10^ 9 \ and a more distant host galaxy with (7 1) 10^ 9 \ and a younger ( 0.1 Gyr) population. The extinction in the foreground galaxy is consistent with zero ( A_V = 0.28^ +0.48 _ 0.28 ), but the host galaxy requires significant reddening ( A_V = 1.62 0.18 ).

We find that the redshift and mass of the foreground galaxy make it fully consistent with a gravitational lens that is capable of satisfying the magnification, image separation limits, and time delay constraints of \ . We thus concluded that \ is not a superluminous supernova but a normal \ magnified by a strong gravitational lens at z=1.1168 .

sources.summary: The host galaxy is recovered through [O II] emission and modeled as a younger, reddened, more distant galaxy with stellar mass 7 +/- 1 x 10^9, whose light competes with the lens.

quotes.quote: We used the Keck-I telescope with the Low-Resolution Imaging Spectrograph (LRIS) with the upgraded red channel to observe the host galaxy and any foreground objects at the sky position of \ on 2013 September 7 (see Fig. fig:slit ) . As illustrated in figure fig:compare_spec , there are two narrow emission features that persist at the location of \ now that the supernova itself has faded away. The [ O 2 ] emission doublet ( = 3726.1,3728.8 \ in the rest frame) from the host galaxy previously identified is clearly recovered (Fig. fig:host_OII ), but we additionally detected a second emission line at about 7890 . Because there are no strong emission lines expected from the host at this wavelength ( 3300 \ in the host frame), this detection suggests the presence of a second object coincident with .

The best-fit SSP combination was a 1 Gyr old foreground galaxy with a stellar mass of (9 2) 10^ 9 \ and a more distant host galaxy with (7 1) 10^ 9 \ and a younger ( 0.1 Gyr) population. The extinction in the foreground galaxy is consistent with zero ( A_V = 0.28^ +0.48 _ 0.28 ), but the host galaxy requires significant reddening ( A_V = 1.62 0.18 ).

gmw_00008291.name: LEDA 212995
gmw_00008291.overview: LEDA 212995 is a small companion galaxy to Mrk 1040 with a nearly matching redshift [arXiv:astro-ph/0402010]. The galaxy exhibits narrow emission lines typical of H II regions, as well as weak absorption lines that suggest it may be partially obscured by Mrk 1040 [arXiv:astro-ph/0402010].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes LEDA 212995 as Mrk 1040's small companion with H II-like narrow emission, nearly matching redshift, weak absorption, and possible obscuration or physical association with Mrk 1040.

quotes.quote: The intensity ratios and widths for the narrow emission lines found in LEDA 212995 are typical of H II regions. The red-shift (0.0169 0.00015) of the object derived from these emission lines is very close to the red-shift of Mrk 1040 (z=0.01665). The weak narrow and broad absorption lines were detected in the H wavelength band of LEDA 212995 spectra. These absorptions indicate that the companion might be at least partly obscured by Mrk 1040.

From this analysis we found that the red-shift of LEDA 212995 corrected to heliocentric is z=0.0169 0.0015 , what is in good agreement with Ward Wilson (1978) and Amram et al. (1992). We have found that the dispersion velocity obtained from emission lines is about 50 km/s, what is also typical for H II regions.

gmw_00008292.name: Carina III
gmw_00008292.overview: Carina III is an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy located near the south celestial pole [arXiv:1804.10230, arXiv:1912.04963]. Its chemical abundances and kinematics suggest it is a galaxy rather than a globular cluster, and it is highly likely associated with the Large Magellanic Cloud system [arXiv:1912.04963, arXiv:1907.09484].

sources.summary: Carina III is identified among the new south-pole dwarfs, observed alongside Carina II, assigned its own non-member comparison set, and linked kinematically to the LMC.

quotes.quote: The most recently-found dwarfs (Carina II, Carina III, and Hydrus I) were identified in imaging surveys near the south celestial pole that are not currently public, so for those objects I use the coordinates and magnitudes provided in the discovery papers.

Carina III (Car III) was observed simultaneously with Car II by li18 , with 4 member stars identified. The stars that are spectroscopically determined to be members of neither Car II nor Car III are mostly located closer to Car II, but in order to provide an astrometric comparison sample of non-members for Car III I consider the same set of 8 stars to be Car III non-members. As with Car II, I remove the reddening correction used by li18 to determine the observed magnitudes of the Car III stars. Car III has 3 confirmed member stars brighter than g=20 . martin07 and hereafter SG07 sg07 both observed Ursa Major II (UMa II) with Keck/DEIMOS.

Viewed in three dimensions and integrated backward in time, the orbits of Hor I, Hyi I, Car III, and Tuc II track that of the LMC quite closely, suggesting that these objects are probably associated with the Magellanic system. deason15 and sales17 both consider Hor I and Tuc II to be the most likely dwarfs of Magellanic origin, which is consistent with the results reported here.

sources.summary: The paper presents first detailed abundances for Carina III, showing it is a galaxy rather than a globular cluster and has abundance trends like other faint dwarfs.

quotes.quote: We present the first detailed elemental abundances in the ultra-faint Magellanic satellite galaxies Carina II (Car II) and Carina III (Car III). With high-resolution Magellan/MIKE spectroscopy, we determined abundances of nine stars in Car II including the first abundances of an RR Lyrae star in an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy; and two stars in Car III. The chemical abundances demonstrate that both systems are clearly galaxies and not globular clusters. The stars in these galaxies mostly display abundance trends matching those of other similarly faint dwarf galaxies: enhanced but declining [ /Fe ] ratios, iron-peak elements matching the stellar halo, and unusually low neutron-capture element abundances.
Two of these LMC satellites are Carina II (Car II, M_V=-4.5 , L/L_ 10^ 3.7 ) and Carina III (Car III, M_V=-2.4 , L/L_ 10^ 2.9 ), discovered in the Magellanic Satellite

sources.summary: The paper finds that including Carina III strengthens dark-matter annihilation limits by about a factor of three, but its uncertain dSph status and J factor broaden the constraints.

quotes.quote: In particular, we note that including Carina III in the analysis strengthens constraints on s wave annihilation into two-body Standard Model final states by a factor of 3 but broadens the error on the constraint due to the large uncertainty of its J factor. Our findings illustrate the importance of verifying if Carina III is in fact a dwarf spheroidal galaxy and measuring more precisely its J factor.

We include Carina III in our analysis but note that there is still significant uncertainty regarding whether or not Carina III is a dSph, since stellar velocity information has been obtained from only four member stars. But because it is relatively nearby, its estimated J factor is the largest of any dSph in the sample we consider. We find that including Carina III in our analysis strengthens our bounds on DM annihilation by a factor of 3 (compared to an analysis with only the other 53 dSphs), but the error on the bound is larger due to the uncertainty in the J factor.

sources.summary: The paper classifies Car 3 among six dwarf galaxies likely accreted with the LMC, noting low-mass binding probability and agreement with a separate simulation-based study.

quotes.quote: We study the orbits of ultra-faint dwarf galaxies in the combined presence of the Milky Way and LMC and we find 6 dwarfs which were likely accreted with the LMC (Car 2, Car 3, Hor 1, Hyi 1, Phe 2, Ret 2), in addition to the SMC, representing strong evidence of dwarf galaxy group infall. Interestingly, some of the LMC satellites (Hor 1, Car 2, Car 3, and the SMC) have a non-zero probability of being bound even with the lowest LMC mass considered suggesting that these sit deep in the potential of the LMC. Using a different technique based on a single cosmological simulation, they identified Car 2, Car 3, Hor 1, and Hyi 1 as likely LMC satellites, in agreement with this work. In addition to the SMC, we identify six dwarf galaxies as being highly likely bound to the Cloud previously: Car 2, Car 3, Hor 1, Hyi 1, Phe 2, and Ret 2.

sources.summary: The paper selects four Carina III members plus one supporting photometric star, finds a clear proper-motion clump, and reports agreement with Simon and Kallivayalil.

quotes.quote: Carina III Again we use the catalog of Li_18a which has binary probabilities. The 4 member stars we select form a clear clump in proper motion space supported by one bright star w/o spectroscopy. Our motion agrees within 1 with Simon_18 and Kallivayalil_18 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies Car3 as one of four ultra-faint dwarf galaxies whose measured 6D kinematics are consistent with membership in the infalling LMC group.

quotes.quote: We conclude that 4 of these galaxies (Hor1, Car2, Car3 and Hyi1) have come in with the Magellanic Clouds, constituting the first confirmation of the type of satellite infall predicted by LCDM.

Hor1, Hyi1, Car3, and Ret2 are clearly consistent with the angular momentum direction of the LMC system, listed in descending order of significance as represented by the density of LMCa debris in the same region.

At least 4 dwarfs show clearly consistent positions and velocity measurements compared to our predictions: Hyi1, Car3, Car2 and Hor1 (red labels), presenting a compelling case of probable membership to the LMC group.

gmw_00008293.name: COSMOS0216+2955
gmw_00008293.overview: COSMOS0216+2955 is an ambiguous candidate for a strong gravitational lens [arXiv:0802.2174]. The system exhibits two potential arcs with a strong color difference from the main galaxy, which may instead be spiral arms linked to the galaxy core [arXiv:0802.2174].

sources.summary: The paper treats COSMOS0216+2955 as an ambiguous lens candidate: color differences suggest arcs, but subtraction residuals may instead be spiral-arm structure tied to the lensing galaxy.

quotes.quote: The pseudo-color image of COSMOS0216+2955 shows a strong color difference between the galaxy and the potential arcs. Nevertheless, the galaxy subtraction shows features that are not fitted by the Sersic profile. They seem to be linked to the galaxy core. Hence, there are two possible explanations for this system: either it is actually a strong lens, and the lensing galaxy is a spiral galaxy, or the two arcs observed are part of the arms of a spiral galaxy.

gmw_00008294.name: COSMOS0254+1430
gmw_00008294.overview: COSMOS0254+1430 is a candidate strong gravitational lensing system that features two lensing galaxies at a very short distance from each other [arXiv:0802.2174]. The system also displays two clearly visible arclets located to the northeast and southwest [arXiv:0802.2174].

sources.summary: The paper describes COSMOS0254+1430 as a candidate system with two nearby lensing galaxies, an unsuccessful clean profile fit, and two visible arclets in the color image.

quotes.quote: The system COSMOS0254+1430 has two lensing galaxies at very short distance. We have not been able to make a clean galaxy light profile fit. The two arclets located North-East and South-West have are clearly visible in the color image.

gmw_00008295.name: SST J1604+4304
gmw_00008295.overview: SST J1604+4304 is an ultraluminous infrared galaxy at a redshift of z = 1.135 [arXiv:0910.2792]. It features a young, metal-rich stellar population [arXiv:0910.2792] and substantial dust [arXiv:0910.2792], with its emission characterized by pure star formation and no active galactic nucleus contribution [arXiv:1011.0511].

sources.summary: The paper presents SST J1604+4304 as a z=1.135 ULIRG with a young, metal-rich, reddened stellar population, large infrared luminosity, substantial dust, and possible obscured starburst or AGN emission.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: R.A. 16 ^h 04 ^m 25 538 decl. +43 04 26 55, in the J2000 system

quotes.quote: We present the detailed optical to far-infrared observations of SST J1604+4304, an ULIRG at z = 1.135 . Analyzing the stellar absorption lines, namely, the CaII H K and Balmer H lines in the optical spectrum, we derive the upper limits of an age for the stellar population. Given this constraint, the minimum ^2 method is used to fit the stellar population models to the observed SED from 0.44 to 5.8 . We find the following properties. The stellar population has an age 40 - 200 Myr with a metallicity 2.5 Z_ .

This is two times greater than that expected from the observed starlight, suggesting either that 1/2 of the starburst site is completely obscured at UV-optical wavelengths, or that 1/2 of L_ ir comes from AGN emission. The inferred dust mass is 2.0 1.0 10^8 M_ . This is sufficient to form a shell surrounding the galaxy with an optical depth E(B-V) = 0.8 .

sources.summary: The paper models SST J1604+4304 as a young z~1 infrared galaxy whose SED favors supernova-dust extinction, pure star formation, and no AGN contribution.

quotes.quote: We show that the SN extinction curves reproduce the data of SST J1604+4304 comparable to or better than the Calzetti extinction curve. The Milky Way extinction curve is not in satisfactory agreement with the data unless several dusty clumps are in the line of sight. This trend may be explained by the abundance of SN-origin dust in these galaxies; SN dust is the most abundant in the young IR galaxy at z 1 , abundant in local starbursts, and less abundant in the Galaxy. If dust in SST J1604+4304 is dominated by SN dust, the dust production rate is 0.1 M _ /SN.
The bolometric luminosity agrees with the extinction-corrected, stellar luminosity, suggesting SST J1604+4304 can be characterized by models of pure star formation with no AGN emission.

gmw_00008296.name: FRBA J0054-2455
gmw_00008296.overview: FRBA J0054-2455 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.04689]. Optical spectroscopy of the source has revealed a featureless spectrum, with only a tentative, unresolved weak feature detected that leaves its redshift undetermined [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper reports new SALT/RSS spectroscopy of FRBA J0054-2455, finding only a tentative weak unresolved feature, no secure identification, and an undetermined redshift.

quotes.quote: FRBA J0054-2455 Two medium S/N optical spectra of this BL Lac have been reported, one by Shaw13 using Keck/LRIS, the other by Mas13 using TNG/DOLORES, and both are featureless. We observed it with SALT/RSS for 2325 s in 2020 December obtaining S/N 100. After careful examination, a possible weak feature is detected around 4750 \ in the integrated spectrum (Fig. fig_spec1 , middle panel, on the left). The putative feature is unresolved, its equivalent width is about 0.4 0.2 , at the sensitivity limit. No identification is possible at this stage.
Given the low S/N of the feature, we consider this a tentative detection. Further observations are needed to investigate this result. The redshift of FRBA J0054-2455 is still undetermined.

gmw_00008297.name: SUMSS J031614-643732
gmw_00008297.overview: SUMSS J031614-643732 is a poorly investigated BL Lacertae object and active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2211.04689]. Spectroscopic observations have detected the Ca II HK doublet of its host galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.6161, indicating a highly luminous host [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper presents SUMSS J031614-643732 as a poorly investigated BL Lac whose SALT/RSS spectrum detects host Ca II HK at z=0.6161 and implies a luminous host galaxy.

quotes.quote: SUMSS J031614-643732 This poorly investigated BL Lac has been observed by Lan15 . We performed three separate observations with SALT/RSS in 2020 December and 2021 January with a total exposure time of 7200 s. The total averaged spectrum is presented in Fig. fig_spec1 , middle panel, on the right. We detected the Ca ii HK doublet of the host galaxy at a significance of more than 5 at z = 0.6161 0.0002, making this one of the farthest blazars in our sample so far. The host galaxy is very luminous with M_ R = -23.4.

gmw_00008298.name: RX J0338.4+1302
gmw_00008298.overview: RX J0338.4+1302 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.04689] and a radio-loud active galaxy [arXiv:1711.05476]. Its optical spectrum displays a mostly featureless continuum [arXiv:1711.05476] with a magnesium II absorption doublet that establishes a spectroscopic redshift of 0.3821 [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper resolves prior redshift evidence for RX J0338.4+1302 by detecting a Mg II absorbing doublet in a Lick/KAST spectrum, setting a lower limit z>=0.3821.

quotes.quote: RX J0338.4+1302 The BL Lac RX J0338.4+1302 lies out of the Galactic plane at b -33 ^ , but is heavily absorbed ( E(B-V) = 0.299, SF11) possibly due to the nearby Galactic dust cloud CODIR 174-34 Dutr02 . A Gran Telescopio Canarias http://www.gtc.iac.es/ (GTC) spectrum Pai17a resulted in the detection of an unresolved absorption feature with EW = 3.0 , which interpreted as Mg ii , indicates z 0.382. The feature is also visible in a previous spectrum by Marc16 .

A Lick/KAST observation was performed for 7200 s. The resulting low S/N spectrum (Fig. fig_spec1 , bottom panel, on the left) is a power law with a clear Mg ii absorbing doublet with EW = 3.0 0.3 around 3870 . We fit the feature with a Mg ii doublet using vpfit Cars14 and obtain a reduced ^2 1.0 for z = 0.3821 0.0002. We therefore determine that the target is at z 0.3821.

sources.summary: The paper discusses 2FGL J0338.2+1306 as a BCU-II blazar candidate, finds one counterpart, and favors an HSP BL Lac solution near redshift 0.3.

quotes.quote: 2FGL J0338.2+1306 (3FGL J0338.5+1303) This source is reported in the 3FGL and 3LAC catalogue as a blazar candidates of uncertain type of the second sub-type (BCU-II) The 3LAC sources classified as blazar candidates of uncertain type are divided in three sub-types: the BCU-I sources where the counterpart has a published optical spectrum but is not sensitive enough for a classification as an FSRQ or a BL Lac; the BCU-II objects whit the counterpart lacking of an optical spectrum but a reliable evaluation of the SED synchrotron-peak position is possible; the BCU-III sources for which the counterpart is lacking both an optical spectrum and an estimated synchrotron-peak position but shows blazar like broadband emission and a flat radio spectrum. and with a detection significance of 11.90 (in the 2FGL it was classified as unassociated). The error-box field is analysed in Sec. 0338_ass , where only one candidate counterpart is found. The broad-band SED of this object is reported in Fig. fig:0338_ass , including the Swift /XRT imaging photometry. The output plots of our blazar recognition tool are shown in Fig. fig:0338_metodo . We have two best-fit solutions for the minimum _ ,min ^ 2 belonging to the HSP class with z=0.3 and z=1.9, the latter having a very large MAD value.

sources.summary: The paper associates 3FGLJ0338.5+1303 with the radio-loud active galaxy RX J0338.1+1302, finding a mostly featureless continuum with an absorption line that sets z > 0.382.

quotes.quote: 3FGLJ0338.5+1303 : In the 3LAC catalog, the source is associated to the radio loud active galaxy RX J0338.1+1302 and classified as BCU-II. The optical spectrum of this object exhibits a featureless continuum except for a clear absorption line at 3867 with an EW = 3.0 . If attributed to Mg II (2800 ), we can set a spectroscopic redshift lower limit of 0.382. A featureless spectrum for this object was also reported by marchesini2016 . Note that the absorption line is also present in their published spectrum but it was not noted and identified.

gmw_00008299.name: SHBL J040324.5-242950
gmw_00008299.overview: SHBL J040324.5-242950 is a blazar with conflicting synchrotron peak classifications that suggest it is either a high-frequency or low-frequency peaked BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.04689]. Spectroscopic observations confirmed its redshift at z = 0.5993 based on the detection of [O III] emission lines [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SHBL J040324.5-242950 at z=0.5993 using [O III] emission, while noting its line strength may make the source more FSRQ-like than BL Lac-like.

quotes.quote: SHBL J040324.5-242950 SHBL J040324.5-242950 is classified as an HBL in the 3HSP catalog Chan19 with _ peak ^ sy = 10 ^ 18 Hz. However, in the 4LAC catalog Fer4LAC20,Fer4LAC22 , _ peak ^ sy = 5.5 10 ^ 12 Hz is quoted, which would suggest it is an LBL. Two redshift values are reported in the literature for SHBL J040324.5-242950: z = 0.599 Heal08 and z = 0.357 Giom05 . To investigate this result, we downloaded the public data of the observation by Heal08 from the ESO archive.

In order to confirm or disprove this result, we observed SHBL J040324.5-242950 with NTT/EFOSC2 using Grism 17, which is sensitive in the range 6895 - 8765 . Despite the non-ideal observing conditions, we were able to secure a low S/N spectrum. The spectrum presents a very faint continuum with S/N = 3 over which we clearly detect at redshift around 0.599 with 10 significance the [ O iii ] b line and with lower significance (4.6 ) the [ O iii ] a line (Fig. fig_spec1 , bottom panel, on the right). Only continuum flux was measured at the position of the H line at this redshift, resulting in a 3 limit of an emission line of 9 .

gmw_00008300.name: SUMSS J050038-491214
gmw_00008300.overview: SUMSS J050038-491214 is a poorly studied BL Lacertae object located at a redshift of z = 0.2129 [arXiv:2211.04689]. Its optical spectrum shows Ca II HK absorption and [O III] emission lines, revealing a particularly faint host galaxy [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper reports first spectroscopy of SUMSS J050038-491214, detecting Ca II HK absorption and [O III] emission at z=0.2129 and finding a comparatively faint host galaxy.

quotes.quote: SUMSS J050038-491214 This poorly studied BL Lac has no previous spectroscopic observation. Inspection of its SED suggests that it may be an HBL but not enough data is available. We observed it with SALT/RSS for 4500 s split in two separate observations both in 2020 January.

On a power-law like spectrum, we detect the Ca ii HK doublet in absorption and [ O iii ] b in emission around z = 0.212 (Fig. fig_spec2 , top panel, on the left). The precise redshift is z = 0.2129 0.0001. The host galaxy is particularly faint at M_ R = -20.5.

gmw_00008301.name: 1ES 0602-48.2
gmw_00008301.overview: 1ES 0602-48.2 is a blazar and BL Lacertae object located at a redshift of z = 0.4542 [arXiv:2211.04689]. Spectroscopic observations of the active galactic nucleus reveal Ca II HK and Ca I G absorption lines, indicating a luminous host galaxy [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper reports that new SALT/RSS spectra of 1ES 0602-48.2 detect Ca II HK and Ca I G at z=0.4542 and estimate a luminous host galaxy.

quotes.quote: 1ES 0602-48.2 A low S/N spectrum of this source was taken by Mas13 with NTT/EFOSC2. No clear feature could be detected. We performed three different observations with SALT/RSS for a total exposure time of 6750 s. In the averaged spectrum (S/N = 72) (Fig. fig_spec2 , middle panel, on the left), we detected Ca ii HK and Ca i G at z = 0.4542 0.0002. The estimated host galaxy magnitude is M_ R = -22.6.

gmw_00008302.name: RX J0819.2-0756
gmw_00008302.overview: RX J0819.2-0756 is a BL Lacertae object, which is a type of active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2211.04689]. Optical spectroscopy of the source has revealed a tentative redshift of approximately 0.320 based on a weak Ca II HK absorption feature [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper treats RX J0819.2-0756 as a BL Lac with conflicting previous redshift estimates and reports a tentative Ca II HK detection near z=0.320.

quotes.quote: RX J0819.2-0756 In the 6dF survey, it is claimed that this source is at z = 0.85115 Jon09 while a photometric redshift z = 0.45 has been estimated by Chan19 . A featureless low S/N spectrum Alv16b was obtained with a short (1200 s) observation using the IMACS medium resolution spectrograph at the 6.5 m Magellan telescope in Cerro Manqui, Chile. We observed the source with NTT/EFOSC2 for 6650 s, obtaining a moderate S/N spectrum.

We observed the source with NTT/EFOSC2 for 6650 s, obtaining a moderate S/N spectrum. In this spectrum, presented in Fig. fig_spec2 , middle panel, on the right, we tentatively detected the Ca ii HK feature at 3.5 at z 0.320. Due to the weakness of the putative feature and the lack of other features, we consider this redshift tentative.

gmw_00008303.name: PMN J1042-0558
gmw_00008303.overview: PMN J1042-0558 is classified as an extreme high-energy peaked BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.04689]. Optical spectroscopy of the active galactic nucleus has detected the Ca II HK doublet, establishing a solid redshift of approximately 0.3925 [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper classifies PMN J1042-0558 as an EHBL and reports NTT/EFOSC2 detection of Ca II HK at z=0.3925, considered reliable despite unavailable corroborating lines.

quotes.quote: PMN J1042-0558 PMN J1042-0558 has been classified as an EHBL Chan19 . A featureless low S/N spectrum has been obtained by Alv16b with the Boller Chivens low resolution spectrograph at the Observatorio Astronomico Nacional 2.1 m telescope in San Pedro Martir (Mexico). We observed it with NTT/EFOSC2 for 3800 s, obtaining a spectrum with S/N = 34.

In the spectrum (Fig. fig_spec2 , bottom panel, on the right), we detected the Ca ii HK feature at z 0.39 at 5.6 . Other possible features could not be detected, as they fall on atmospheric absorption or they are out of the spectral range. Given the reliability of the detection of the Ca ii HK doublet however, we consider this a solid result.

gmw_00008304.name: NVSS J113046-31380
gmw_00008304.overview: NVSS J113046-31380 is an extended optical source [arXiv:2211.04689] classified as a BL Lacertae object and a high-synchrotron-peaked blazar [arXiv:2104.13333]. Its optical spectrum is dominated by galaxy emission with a weak power-law component [arXiv:2211.04689], placing the galaxy at a redshift of approximately 0.15 [arXiv:2104.13333, arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper detects H and O iii emission in 3FHL J1130.7 3137, derives z=0.15, and says 6dF independently found the same redshift and BL Lac classification.

quotes.quote: The optical spectrum of this source displayed two emission features at 5609 and 5770 , corresponding to H (EW = 2.4 ) and O iii (EW = 1.1 ), respectively. This yields a redshift of z= 0.15 for the source. The 6dF Galaxy Survey 6df obtained a spectrum for this source (obtained by the 6dF fibre-fed multi-object spectrograph at the United Kingdom Schmidt Telescope) from which they derived a redshift of 0.15, and classified the source as a BL Lac, in agreement with our results. This source has been classified as an HSP blazar in 4LAC.

sources.summary: The paper reports that NVSS J113046-31380 is an extended optical source whose galaxy-dominated NTT/EFOSC2 spectrum confirms a previous possible redshift at z=0.1507.

quotes.quote: NVSS J113046-31380 NVSS J113046-31380 is an extended optical source found in the 2MASX catalogue Jar00 , with a possible redshift z = 0.15 Jon09 . We obtained a medium S/N spectrum with NTT/EFOSC2, which is dominated by galaxy emission at z 0.15 with a weak power law component (Fig. fig_spec3 , top panel, on the left). Averaging on the detected features, we obtain z = 0.1507 0.0003, confirming with higher precision the previous result.

gmw_00008305.name: NVSS J125949-37485
gmw_00008305.overview: NVSS J125949-37485 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.04689] located at a redshift of z = 0.2107 [arXiv:2211.04689]. Its optical spectrum displays absorption features, including Ca II HK and Mg b, which became more detectable during a period when its non-thermal component weakened [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper refines NVSS J125949-37485 to z=0.2108 by combining tentative NTT features with stronger SALT/RSS detections, likely helped by a dimmer nonthermal state.

quotes.quote: NVSS J125949-37485 observed this BL Lac with the Goodman spectrograph on the SOAR telescope obtaining a low S/N spectrum without any detectable feature. We first observed the source with NTT/EFOSC2, obtaining a moderate S/N spectrum (Fig. fig_spec3 , top panel, on the right). In this spectrum, we tentatively detect at z 0.211 the Ca ii HK doublet at 3 and Ca i G at 5 .

In order to confirm or disprove this result, we observed the source with SALT/RSS and again secured a medium S/N spectrum (Fig. fig_spec3 , middle panel, on the left). In this case, however, we were able to detect Ca ii HK at 8 and Mg _b at 9 . We thus were able to determine the redshift of NVSS J125949-37485 as z = 0.2107 0.0002, fully compatible with but more precise than the NTT/EFOSC2 result. Despite the fact that the EFOSC2 and RSS spectra have similar continuum S/N, we think that we were able to better detect the absorption features in the RSS spectrum due to a slight weakening of the non-thermal component. This is supported by the estimated magnitudes, which show that the source was brighter during the EFOSC2 observation (see Table tabres1 ).

gmw_00008306.name: 1RXS J130421.2-43508
gmw_00008306.overview: 1RXS J130421.2-43508 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.04689]. Its redshift remains unknown because multiple spectroscopic observations have yielded only featureless spectra with no detectable spectral features [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper reports that 1RXS J130421.2-43508 remains at unknown redshift because both earlier NTT/EFOSC2 data and a new high-S/N SALT/RSS spectrum are featureless.

quotes.quote: 1RXS J130421.2-43508 This BL Lac was observed with NTT/EFOSC2 by Mas13 , obtaining a medium S/N featureless spectrum. We then observed it with SALT/RSS for 2100 s. The resulting spectrum with S/N 160 presented in Fig. fig_spec3 , middle panel, on the right, has no detectable spectral features. The redshift of 1RXS J130421.2-43508 thus remains unknown.

gmw_00008307.name: TXS 1530-131
gmw_00008307.overview: TXS 1530-131 is a variable gamma-ray blazar classified as a low-frequency peaked BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.04689]. The source exhibits weak optical emission and strong gamma-ray variability, but its optical spectrum remains difficult to characterize due to its low signal-to-noise ratio [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper describes TXS 1530-131 as a variable gamma-ray LBL with weak optical emission; three SALT/RSS observations produced only a very low-S/N spectrum and no redshift.

quotes.quote: TXS 1530-131 TXS 1530-131 was first detected in gamma rays by Fermi LAT during a strong flare in 2011 Gasp11 . Its Fermi LAT light curve https://fermi.gsfc.nasa.gov/ssc/data/access/lat/msl_lc shows a strong variability. Its optical emission is quite weak Kau17 with i^ = 18.70 0.03 and no optical spectrum has been reported. We inspected its SED and found that the synchrotron peak is at around 10 ^ 13 Hz, suggesting that the source is an LBL.

We observed TXS 1530-131 three different times with SALT/RSS during 2020 July and August for a total exposure time of 6750 s. The source was very weak with R_ = 22.3 0.2, more than three magnitudes weaker than during the Kau17 observations. Given the weakness of the source, we could only obtain a very low S/N = 5 (Fig. fig_spec3 , bottom panel, on the right) spectrum.

gmw_00008308.name: NVSS J154952-065907
gmw_00008308.overview: NVSS J154952-065907 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.04689], which is a type of active galactic nucleus. Keck/ESI spectroscopy of the source detects multiple host absorption features at a redshift of z = 0.4187, indicating a luminous host galaxy [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper reports that Keck/ESI spectroscopy of NVSS J154952-065907 detects multiple host absorption features at z=0.4187, consistent with previous work and implying a luminous host.

quotes.quote: NVSS J154952-065907 NVSS J154952-065907 was observed by Marc16 using the DOLORES spectrograph at the Telescopio Nazionale Galileo in the Canary Islands. They obtained a medium S/N spectrum with no lines. We observed this BL Lac with Keck/ESI for 7200 s, securing a spectrum with S/N of 101. We detect with high confidence Ca ii HK, Ca i G, Mg _b and Ca Fe at z = 0.4187 0.0005 (Fig. fig_spec4 , top panel, on the left). This result is consistent with the one of Pai20 .

gmw_00008309.name: 1RXS J171921.2+120711
gmw_00008309.overview: 1RXS J171921.2+120711 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.04689] with a weak optical counterpart [arXiv:2211.04689]. Spectroscopic observations of the active galactic nucleus detected no features, leaving its redshift unknown [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper reports exploratory and Keck/ESI spectroscopy of 1RXS J171921.2+120711, but no features were detected and the BL Lac redshift remains unknown.

quotes.quote: 1RXS J171921.2+120711 1RXS J171921.2+120711 has a rather weak optical counterpart with i = 18.3 in the PanSTARRS survey Mag20 . We could not find any optical spectrum of this BL Lac in the literature, and we therefore performed an exploratory observation with Lick/KAST. Only a low S/N spectrum could be obtained (see Table tabobslick ), which showed that a more powerful instrument was needed.

Only a low S/N spectrum could be obtained (see Table tabobslick ), which showed that a more powerful instrument was needed. We then took a spectrum with Keck/ESI, observing for 7200 s. In the resulting spectrum with S/N 85, no feature could be detected (Fig. fig_spec4 , top panel, on the right). The redshift of 1RXS J171921.2+120711 remains unknown.

gmw_00008310.name: NVSS J184425+15464
gmw_00008310.overview: NVSS J184425+15464 is a blazar located near the Galactic plane [arXiv:2211.04689]. Although previously assigned a photometric redshift of 0.11, the detection of a magnesium II absorption system in its spectrum indicates the object is actually at a redshift of at least 0.6293 [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper contradicts a photometric redshift for NVSS J184425+15464 by detecting a saturated Mg II absorber in a Lick/KAST spectrum and setting z>=0.6293.

quotes.quote: NVSS J184425+15464 NVSS J184425+15464 is located near the Galactic plane at b +8.6 ^ and is very absorbed with E(B-V) = 0.3914. Two low S/N spectra were taken Mas15a, Alv16b with the Boller Chivens low resolution spectrograph at the Observatorio Astronomico Nacional 2.1 m telescope in San Pedro Martir (Mexico) but no features could be detected. A photometric redshift z = 0.11 Chan19 has been reported.

We then observed it with Lick/KAST for 7200 s. A low S/N spectrum was obtained (Fig. fig_spec4 , middle panel, on the left), consisting of a flat power law on which a Mg ii absorbing system with EW = 1.3 0.2 around 4550 is visible. We fitted the feature with a Mg ii doublet using vpfit Cars14 , obtaining a reduced ^2 1.0 for z = 0.6293 0.0006. We therefore determine that the target is at z 0.6293, in contradiction to the photometric redshift quoted above.

gmw_00008311.name: 1RXS J193320.3+072616
gmw_00008311.overview: The active galactic nucleus 1RXS J193320.3+072616 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.04689]. Although recent imaging suggests the source may be extended, its redshift remains undetermined because multiple spectra have failed to reveal any extragalactic features [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper reports that 1RXS J193320.3+072616 may be extended, but medium- and high-S/N spectra show no extragalactic features, leaving its redshift undetermined.

quotes.quote: 1RXS J193320.3+072616 A medium S/N featureless spectrum taken at Telescopio Nazionale Galileo with the DOLORES spectrograph of this heavily absorbed E(B-V) = 0.2539 source has been reported by Mas13 . Recent imaging observations have shown that it may be extended (Fallah Ramazani et. al in preparation). We performed two preliminary observations with Lick/KAST in 2019, obtaining medium S/N spectra (Table tabobslick ) but could not detect any feature. In 2020 July, a 5400 s observation was performed with Keck/ESI, resulting in a spectrum with S/N = 110 (Fig. fig_spec4 , middle panel, on the right). Unfortunately no extragalactic features could be detected in this spectrum and the redshift of 1RXS J193320.3+072616 remains undetermined.

gmw_00008312.name: RX J2030.8+1935
gmw_00008312.overview: RX J2030.8+1935 is a BL Lacertae object located at a redshift of approximately z = 0.366 [arXiv:2211.04689, arXiv:2302.10080]. Its optical spectrum displays a non-thermal continuum with weak [O II] and [O III] emission lines, alongside Ca II absorption features from its host galaxy's stellar population [arXiv:2211.04689, arXiv:2302.10080].

sources.summary: The paper overturns an earlier tentative Mg II interpretation for RX J2030.8+1935 by detecting multiple host and emission features at z=0.3665 and finding a luminous host.

quotes.quote: RX J2030.8+1935 Mas15a reported a low S/N spectrum of this source, taken with the Boller Chivens low resolution spectrograph at the Observatorio Astronomico Nacional 2.1 m telescope in San Pedro Martir (Mexico). They tentatively detected a broad emission line around 4670 , which, if interpreted as Mg ii , would put the source at z 0.668. In 2019, we performed an exploratory observation with Lick/KAST, obtaining a featureless spectrum with S/N = 12 (see Table tabobslick ).

In order to obtain a high S/N spectrum, we then observed it with Keck/ESI for 2 hours obtaining a S/N = 98. In the resulting spectrum, we identify Ca ii HK, Ca i G, Mg _b , [ O ii ] and [ O iii ] b at z = 0.3665 0.0005 (Fig. fig_spec4 , bottom panel, on the left). We thus contradict the interpretation of the previous result. The host galaxy is quite luminous at M_ R = -23.5.

sources.summary: 4FGL J2030.9+1935 is a BLL-like source with [O II], weak [O III], and Ca II host absorption at z = 0.3662, though imaging does not detect its host.

quotes.quote: 4FGL J2030.9+1935 : In our good (S/N 150) optical spectrum, showing the typical BLL non-thermal continuum, we found one emission line at 5092  (EW 0.50  ) attributed to [ O II ] and another very weak (EW 0.2  ) emission feature at 6840  consistent with [ O III ] , yielding z = 0.3662. The G-band is overlapped to the Galactic Na I absorption line. From the spectral decomposition analysis (see Fig.  fig:decomposition ) we get N/H = 14. In addition, at the same redshift we detect the absorption Ca II doublet due to stellar population of its host galaxy. However, from the analysis of the GTC acquisition image, we are not able to detect the host galaxy ( M(R) > -22.6 ).

gmw_00008313.name: NVSS J224604+15443
gmw_00008313.overview: NVSS J224604+15443 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.04689] associated with the gamma-ray source 3FGLJ2246.2+1547 [arXiv:1711.05476]. While early optical spectroscopy showed a featureless spectrum [arXiv:1711.05476], subsequent observations detected emission and absorption lines that confirm a redshift of z = 0.5966 [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper confirms NVSS J224604+15443 as a BL Lac/IBL at z=0.5966 using Keck/ESI detections of [O II], Ca II HK, and [O III].

quotes.quote: NVSS J224604+15443 This source is classified as a BCU in the 3FHL catalogue. Inspection of its SED suggests that it is an IBL. Two medium S/N spectra have been taken by Pai17a,Pai19 and both support its classification as a BL Lac. In the first spectrum, a weak feature, contaminated by telluric absorption, is present around 6300 .

We decided to perform a Keck/ESI observation to confirm or disprove this value. In a short 1800 s observation, we were able to detect [ O ii ] , Ca ii HK and [ O iii ] b with high confidence at z = 0.5966 0.0003, confirming with higher precision the previous result (Fig. fig_spec5 , on the left).

sources.summary: The paper associates 3FGLJ2246.2+1547 with NVSSJ224604+154437, confirms a BL Lac-type featureless spectrum, and sets a redshift lower limit of z > 0.7.

quotes.quote: 3FGLJ2246.2+1547 This ray emitter is associated to the radio source NVSSJ224604+154437 and classified as BCU-II in the 3LAC catalog. No optical spectra were previously found in the literature. Our spectrum of the optical counterpart confirms the BLL nature of the source and exhibits a pure featureless spectrum. We can set a lower limit of the redshift of z > 0.7 (see Tab. tab:table3 ).

gmw_00008314.name: PMN J2321-6438
gmw_00008314.overview: PMN J2321-6438 is an active galactic nucleus classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.04689]. Spectral observations place the source at a redshift of z = 0.8126 [arXiv:2211.04689], and its spectral energy distribution suggests it is an intermediate-synchrotron-peaked BL Lac [arXiv:2211.04689].

sources.summary: The paper confirms PMN J2321-6438 as a BL Lac at z=0.8126 after SALT/RSS spectra reveal Mg II absorption systems and identify the earlier ambiguous emission line as [O II].

quotes.quote: PMN J2321-6438 PMN J2321-6438 is classified as a BCU in the 3FHL catalogue. It has been identified as a BL Lac with a low S/N featureless spectrum obtained by Des19 with the COSMOS spectrograph at the Blanco telescope in Chile. Inspection of its SED suggests that it is an IBL. We observed it with SALT/RSS the first time for 2250 s, obtaining a S/N = 43 spectrum. A single narrow ( 450 km s ^ 1 ) emission line is clearly visible in the spectrum at around 6755 , detected at 6 (Fig. fig_spec5 , on the right, right inset).

These detections clearly establish that the source is at z 0.7901, and determines that the emission line previously detected is very likely [ O ii ] . The redshift of PMN J2321-6438 is thus z = 0.8126 0.0002. Additionally, the absence of bright emission lines confirms the Des19 identification of PMN J2321-6438 as a BL Lac.

gmw_00008315.name: J1200
gmw_00008315.overview: J1200 is a compact star-forming galaxy selected from the CLASSY sample [arXiv:2308.14489]. It exhibits clear evidence for very massive stars and possesses an unusually high electron density, indicating that intense star formation is occurring over short timescales in dense regions [arXiv:2308.14489].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1200 as a compact CLASSY star-forming galaxy with clear VMS evidence; models reproduce most features, and CLASSY selected it for unusually high electron density.

quotes.quote: Beyond R136, the two galaxies with clear evidence for VMS are J1129, previously studied by , who suggested the possible presence of VMS in this galaxy, and J1200, a compact star-forming galaxy from the CLASSY sample berg22 .

The right panel of Fig. fig_j1129vms shows the comparison of CSF models for the other VMS source, J1200. The conclusions are similar, and the models reproduce most features relatively well. The observed spectrum is rather noisy in the blue bump region, although broad \ emission can be excluded.

Furthermore, J1200 was selected by CLASSY as an object with a high electron density ( n_e > 400 cm ^ 3 ), which is very rare at low redshifts berg22 . This indicates that intense star formation occurs over short timescales and in fairly compact and/or dense regions in these VMS-hosting objects.

gmw_00008316.name: NG
gmw_00008316.overview: NG is a dwarf galaxy with an average radial velocity of 6246 km/s [arXiv:1909.05797]. It exhibits H-alpha emission, a galactic rotation of approximately 60 km/s, and is expected to interact with the Stephan's Quintet system in the future [arXiv:1909.05797].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NG as a new SQ dwarf galaxy with H emission, spatially resolved velocity maps, rotation of about 60 km/s, and an average radial velocity of 6246 km/s.

quotes.quote: In Figs. fig:NG and fig:NG7320c we present the spatially resolved H flux and radial velocity maps for NG and NGC7320c, respectively. We detect H emission in NG. This, combined with the fact that NG is extended and presents a galactic rotation of 60 km s^ 1 as shown in Fig. fig:NG e.g. 2002A&A...390..829S,2013MNRAS.428.1743W , indicates that NG is consistent with a dwarf galaxy that will end up interacting with the SQ system in the future, similar to M82 (according to the Tully-Fisher relation).

A more detailed study has been made for NG. In Fig. fig:NG we show the H flux (left panel) and the radial velocity map (right panel) of NG. The inner panel shows the Hubble Space Telescope optical imaging of NG. The velocity gradient is clearly seen from the south to the north (v 6300 to 6150 km s^ 1 ). As we can see in Table table:table2 , region 176, the average radial velocity for NG is 6246 km s^ 1 .

gmw_00008317.name: QSO B0516 621
gmw_00008317.overview: QSO B0516 621 is a blazar active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2102.05833]. Its high-energy spectrum displays a persistent, line-like feature near 7 GeV that cannot be explained by standard jet radiation processes [arXiv:2102.05833].

sources.summary: The paper reports a persistent 7 GeV line-like feature in blazar QSO B0516 621 and models it as possible photon-ALP oscillation in the jet.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we report a line-like feature found in the spectrum of a blazar, QSO B0516 621 (e.g., ref. ). Standard radiation processes considered for jets' emission cannot explain this feature. After our thorough searches for possible explanations, we realized that it can be considered as a photon-ALP oscillation signal. Detailed studies of this case was conducted.

The events of Pass 8 `SOURCE' class (evclass=128, evtype=3) within a 20^ 20^ region of interest (RoI) were selected, which is centered at the target B0516 621 (the \ LAT counterpart is named as 4FGL J0516.7 6207).

Spectral analysis We obtained the spectrum of B0516 621 by performing the maximum likelihood analysis of the LAT data in evenly divided energy bins in logarithm. There were few photons above 60 GeV, and we found 30 bins between 0.1--60 GeV allow to show the spectrum relatively well without having many flux upper limits at the high-energy end. The obtained spectrum is shown in Figure fig:spec , and a line-like feature is seen near 7 GeV.

gmw_00008318.name: ID 355
aliases_external: Newman et al. JKCS 041 member ID 355
gmw_00008318.overview: ID 355 is a high-redshift passive galaxy [arXiv:2202.04086]. It exhibits a negative metallicity gradient with solar and supersolar metallicities in its inner zone, and it uniquely displays a negative age gradient with an older inner stellar population than its outskirts [arXiv:2202.04086].

sources.summary: ID 355 is one of four successfully analyzed z~2 passive galaxies; it has negative metallicity gradients and uniquely shows an older inner stellar population than its outskirts.

quotes.quote: In the following, we present our results for the four galaxies with the smallest errors (ID 355 , ID 272 , ID 657 , and ID 64 ). They are also presented in Table  table:selected . Figure  fig:agemet summarises our results for the four galaxies. It reports the median value of age and metallicity for each zone and their 68 probability intervals. We find solar and supersolar metallicities in the inner zones of all the galaxies and subsolar values in the outer zones, indicating negative metallicity gradients in all four galaxies.

Three out of the four galaxies the inner and outer zone have comparable ages, while ID 355 presents an older stellar population in the inner zone than in the outskirts. Only one of the four galaxies, namely ID 355 , displays a negative age gradient.

gmw_00008319.name: ID 272
aliases_external: Newman et al. JKCS 041 member ID 272
gmw_00008319.overview: ID 272 is a high-redshift passive galaxy that has been successfully analyzed with spatially resolved age-metallicity measurements [arXiv:2202.04086]. The galaxy exhibits a negative metallicity gradient, which is consistent with the gradients observed in local early-type galaxies [arXiv:2202.04086].

sources.summary: ID 272 is one of four high-S/N passive galaxies with successful spatially resolved age-metallicity measurements, showing a negative metallicity gradient consistent with local ETG gradients.

quotes.quote: In the following, we present our results for the four galaxies with the smallest errors (ID 355 , ID 272 , ID 657 , and ID 64 ). They are also presented in Table  table:selected . Figure  fig:agemet summarises our results for the four galaxies. It reports the median value of age and metallicity for each zone and their 68 probability intervals. We find solar and supersolar metallicities in the inner zones of all the galaxies and subsolar values in the outer zones, indicating negative metallicity gradients in all four galaxies.

The four galaxies for which the analysis was successful (ID 355 , ID 272 , ID 657, and ID 64 ) show negative metallicity gradients, although at only 1 . Although the sample is small, the results we obtained and the comparison with metallicity and age gradients measured in the local Universe tend to support the revised monolithic scenario as the more probable paradigm for ETGs formation and evolution. We found metallicity gradients consistent with those confirmed at z=0 and z=0.8 .

gmw_00008320.name: ID 657
aliases_external: Newman et al. JKCS 041 member ID 657
gmw_00008320.overview: ID 657 is a high-redshift, passive galaxy that is fainter than typical cluster galaxies [arXiv:2202.04086]. Despite its lower brightness, its surface brightness and dimensions allow for successful structural analysis, which reveals a negative metallicity gradient [arXiv:2202.04086].

sources.summary: ID 657 is a borderline but successful passive-galaxy case, fainter than the cluster galaxies yet suitable for analysis and showing the common negative metallicity gradient.

quotes.quote: Galaxy ID 657 represents a borderline case. It has one of the highest S/Ns in the inner zone and the best S/N in the outer zone of the other five galaxies. These four galaxies are characterised by the highest S/N in the outer regions (filled symbols) and are those for which our analysis obtained the smallest errors when we estimated age and metallicity in the two zones.

Althoug ID657 is fainter, it has the right combination of surface brightness and dimensions in order to be successfully analysed. The four galaxies for which the analysis was successful (ID 355 , ID 272 , ID 657, and ID 64 ) show negative metallicity gradients, although at only 1 .

gmw_00008321.name: ID 64
aliases_external: Newman et al. JKCS 041 object ID 64
gmw_00008321.overview: ID 64 is a high-redshift quenched galaxy [arXiv:2202.04086]. It exhibits a negative metallicity gradient, characterized by solar and supersolar metallicities in its inner zones and subsolar values in its outer zones [arXiv:2202.04086].

sources.summary: ID 64 is the higher-redshift successful galaxy; the paper uses its H/H/G-band rest-frame range and finds the same negative metallicity-gradient behavior as the other analyzed galaxies.

quotes.quote: For the higher-redshift galaxy ID 64 , we considered the rest-frame range that includes H and H lines (sensitive to the stellar age) and G band (4300) (which is also sensitive to stellar metallicity). Figure  fig:agemet summarises our results for the four galaxies. It reports the median value of age and metallicity for each zone and their 68 probability intervals. We find solar and supersolar metallicities in the inner zones of all the galaxies and subsolar values in the outer zones, indicating negative metallicity gradients in all four galaxies.

The four galaxies for which the analysis was successful (ID 355 , ID 272 , ID 657, and ID 64 ) show negative metallicity gradients, although at only 1 . Although the sample is small, the results we obtained and the comparison with metallicity and age gradients measured in the local Universe tend to support the revised monolithic scenario as the more probable paradigm for ETGs formation and evolution. We found metallicity gradients consistent with those confirmed at z=0 and z=0.8 .

gmw_00008322.name: SDSS J143359.16+400636.0
gmw_00008322.overview: SDSS J143359.16+400636.0 is an elliptical galaxy located at a redshift of 0.099 [arXiv:2010.12587]. Its nucleus exhibits LINER activity, though there was no evidence of an active galactic nucleus in the infrared prior to a detected X-ray transient [arXiv:2010.12587].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes SDSS J143359.16+400636.0 as a z=0.099 elliptical host with LINER activity, no powerful infrared AGN evidence before the flare, and a stellar-dispersion black hole mass log(M/Msun)=7.41.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The \ position of the nucleus is R.A. = 14h 33m 59.170s, Decl.=+40 \ 06 \ 36.05 \ (J2000).

quotes.quote: The The redshift of the galaxy was also determined to be 0.099. The velocity dispersion of the stellar absorption lines was determined to be 213 12 . We used this to calculate the black hole mass from the _ * relation, using the fit to the reverberation-mapped AGN sample from , and the following formula, log( / ) = + log ( _ * /200 ), where =7.31 0.15 and = 3.46 0.61 . The intrinsic scatter of this relation is = 0.41 0.05 . This yielded log( / ) =7.41 0.41 .

No morphological type for the galaxy is reported in the literature, however, our Tractor fitting of the ZTF images finds that its brightness profile was better fit by a deVaucoleur profile than an exponential profile, implying that it is an elliptical galaxy. The WISE colors of W1 W2=0.24 are less than the W1 W2 0.8 selection criterion of for AGN, meaning there was no evidence for the presence of a powerful AGN from the infrared in the galaxy prior to the X-ray transient. However, as described in Section sec_keck , the optical line ratios revealed LINER activity in the nucleus.

gmw_00008323.name: SDSS J143357.57+400647.3
gmw_00008323.overview: SDSS J143357.57+400647.3 is a galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of 0.0990 [arXiv:2010.12587]. It is a brighter, visually larger, and likely more massive companion to a nearby tidal disruption event host galaxy, with which it is likely interacting [arXiv:2010.12587].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J143357.57+400647.3 as a likely interacting, brighter, more massive companion to the TDE host, separated by 21 arcsec or 38 kpc at the same redshift.

quotes.quote: also has a companion galaxy, SDSS J143357.57+400647.3, which has an angular separation of 21 \ and has spectroscopic redshift of 0.0990 from SDSS. This angular distance corresponds to a projected separation of 38 kpc at this redshift meaning that the two galaxies are likely interacting. The companion is brighter and visually larger on the sky, implying it is the more massive of the two.

gmw_00008324.name: J1027 + 0112
gmw_00008324.overview: J1027 + 0112 is a radio source that is likely a background active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2405.00784]. It is coincident with a red, point-like optical counterpart [arXiv:2405.00784].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1027 + 0112 as a likely background AGN, based on a red point-like optical counterpart, removes it from the FRB-PRS candidate sample, and uses it for comparison.

quotes.quote: We find no apparent optical sources at the locations of our sources except for source J1027 + 0112, which is coincident with an apparently red point-like source with a dereddened and z band brightness of 23.96 AB mag and 21.88 AB mag, respectively. Based on the spatial coincidence and color, we conclude that source J1027 + 0112 is likely a background AGN unconnected to the dwarf galaxy. We thus remove the source from our sample as a potential FRB-PRS candidate, and instead use it as a source for comparison.

gmw_00008325.name: J1220 + 3020
gmw_00008325.overview: J1220+3020 is a dwarf galaxy [arXiv:2101.12217, arXiv:2405.00784] that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2101.12217, arXiv:2405.00784]. This active nucleus is confirmed by coronal line emission [arXiv:2101.12217, arXiv:2405.00784] and is associated with extended, steep-spectrum radio emission [arXiv:2405.00784].

sources.summary: The paper uses IFU data to confirm an active black hole in dwarf galaxy J1220+3020, finding coronal-line emission, broad H emission, a nuclear radio source, and AGN-like line ratios.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Galaxy R.A. b & 185.04692 & degrees Galaxy Dec. b & 30.33562 & degrees

quotes.quote: We focus on the dwarf galaxy J1220+3020, which shows no clear optical AGN signatures in its nuclear SDSS spectrum covering the radio source. With our new IFU data, we confirm the presence of an active BH via the AGN coronal line [ Fe 10 ] and enhanced [ O 1 ] emission coincident with the radio source. Furthermore, we detect broad H emission and estimate a BH mass of M_ BH =10^ 4.9 M_ . We compare the narrow emission line ratios to standard BPT diagnostics and shock models. Spatially-resolved BPT diagrams show some AGN signatures, particularly in [ O 1 ] /H , but overall do not unambiguously identify the AGN.

In this paper, we present the results for SDSS J122011.26+302008.1 (ID 82 in reines2020 ), hereafter referred to as J1220+3020. The Dark Energy Camera Legacy Survey DECaLS; decals optical image and the VLA radio emission for this galaxy are shown in Figure  fig:gal , and the galaxy properties are listed in Table  table:gprop . The VLA observation for J1220+3020 was consistent with a point source at a resolution of 0 18 , which corresponds to 100  pc.

sources.summary: The paper treats J1220 + 3020 as a confirmed AGN with coronal [Fe X] and enhanced [O I] emission, extended steep-spectrum radio emission, and a VLBA non-detection because it is resolved out.

quotes.quote: More recently, among the parent sample of 13 radio sources in dwarf galaxies, J1220 + 3020 and J1136 + 1252 have been confirmed as AGN based on spectroscopic data. In particular, J1220 + 3020 shows the presence of coronal line [ Fe X ] and enhanced [ O I ] emission Molina21 , while J1136 + 1252 is associated with an optical counterpart in deep Hubble Space Telescope ( HST ) imaging at a spectroscopic redshift of z = 0.76 (Sturm et al. 2024, in prep. ), pointing to a background quasar origin.
The non-detection of the AGN source J1220 + 3020 with VLBA indicates that the radio source is effectively ``resolved out'', exhibiting a physical extent beyond the LAS of the VLBA observations.
This interpretation is further reinforced by their similarity to the known AGN source J1220 + 3020 in our sample, which exhibits a steep spectral index and extended radio emission.

gmw_00008326.name: SWIRE2 J021749.00-052306.9
gmw_00008326.overview: SWIRE2 J021749.00-052306.9 is a narrow-line quasar located at a redshift of z=0.914 [arXiv:0902.2517]. Its spectrum exhibits strong narrow emission lines, including [OIII], H-alpha, and [NII] [arXiv:0902.2517].

sources.summary: The paper reports near-infrared spectroscopy of SWIRE2 J021749.00-052306.9, measuring z=0.914 from narrow emission lines and classifying it as a narrow-line quasar.

quotes.quote: SWIRE2 J021559.46-045517.5 has been observed for a total of 1200 sec and SWIRE2 J021749.00-052306.9 for a total of 1800 sec.

SWIRE2 J021749.00-052306.9 (No. 6): We obtained a redshift of z=0.914 on the basis of the identification of strong [ OIII ] 4959, 5007 , H _ 6563 and [ NII ] 6583 narrow emission lines (see Fig. 2b). This value does not match with the tentative redshift of z=0.987 proposed by Lacy et al. (2007), using very low S/N optical spectral data. The FWHM of the H _ line is FWHM < 1000 km s ^ 1 . This support a narrow line quasar classification for this .

gmw_00008327.name: C1
gmw_00008327.overview: C1 is an early-type elliptical galaxy with a redshift of 0.1276 [arXiv:1603.02397]. Its spectrum shows stellar absorption features and no emission lines, indicating an old stellar population with red colors [arXiv:1603.02397].

sources.summary: The paper identifies C1 as an old early-type companion candidate, measures z=0.1276 from its spectrum, and estimates its V-band luminosity and red colors while noting weak age constraints.

quotes.quote: The spectrum of C1 galaxy shows characteristics of an early-type galaxy (ETG) of old stellar population, with visible stellar absorption features and no emission lines. By comparing the C1 spectrum with the template spectra in the SWIRE template library 2007ApJ...663...81P , a best-matched template of elliptical galaxy of 5 Gyr old is picked up. Because of no strong emission lines, we perform a grid search of redshift for the redshift of C1.
The redshift value corresponding to the smallest ^ 2 is taken as the best-fit redshift of the C1 galaxy, which is 0.1276 with the 1- error of 0.0004 ( ^ 2 = 1 ).

We also calculate the V band luminosity for C1 by convolving the spectrum with Jonson V band filter, yielding L_ V = 7.8 10^ 42 . Though the best-matched template for C1 shows a stellar age of 5 Gyr, the real stellar age of C1 is hard to determine with the low signal-to-noise ratio(S/N) spectrum. The colors ( g- , i ) of C1 from the photometry are (1.03, 0.34), which are even redder than that of .

gmw_00008328.name: KKH 57
gmw_00008328.overview: KKH 57 is a small, low-luminosity dwarf galaxy located in a relatively isolated region of the M81 group [arXiv:astro-ph/0507168]. It is classified as a transition dwarf galaxy that contains unresolved neutral hydrogen [arXiv:0708.2836] and exhibits ongoing star formation [arXiv:astro-ph/0507168].

sources.summary: The VLA observations detect unresolved HI coincident with KKH 57, giving it a low HI-to-light ratio consistent with a transition dwarf and no other field clouds.

quotes.quote: The results of our observations can be summarised as follows: KKH 57 We find a clear detection coinciding with the optical counterpart. The HI signal is only four channels wide at a systemic velocity of 203 km s ^ 1 ; spatially the HI is unresolved. The low M _ HI /L suggests this is a transition galaxy, i.e., an object which combines properties of a dSph, such as a low luminosity and prominent old stellar population, with those of a dIrr, e.g., the presence of HI . No other clouds are detected in this field.

sources.summary: The paper reports Gemini-N NIRI analysis of kkh57, a low-luminosity isolated M81-group dSph with upper-AGB stars and evidence for ongoing star formation.

quotes.quote: Gemini-N NIRI imaging has now also been analysed for an additional M81 group dSph, known as kkh57 (Da Costa et al., in preparation). This dSph is a small, low luminosity (M _ V -10.9) system, that lies in a rather isolated location in the group, approximately 370kpc in projection from M81 . The -m diagram for kkh57 clearly shows the presence of upper-AGB stars, indicating on-going star formation in this dSph as well. The brightest of the upper-AGB stars have M _ bol -4.3 to --4.6, somewhat more luminous than for BK 5N yet fainter than for F8D1 .

gmw_00008329.name: KDG 61
gmw_00008329.overview: KDG 61 is a transition-type dwarf spheroidal galaxy located close to M81 [arXiv:1003.0861, arXiv:1104.5318]. It contains a metal-poor stellar population [arXiv:1003.0861], though nearby HI clouds and a bright HII region are likely projected from M81 rather than being physically associated with the dwarf itself [arXiv:0708.2836, arXiv:1104.5318].

sources.summary: KDG 61 is placed in the M81 transition region, classified as a transition-type dwarf with HI or H emission, closest to M81, and among dwarfs with metal-poor populations.

quotes.quote: The M 81 group dSphs that seem to lie in this apparent transition region are KDG 61, KDG 64, DDO 44 and DDO 71. Out of these four objects, three are classified as transition-types, namely, KDG 61, KDG 64 and DDO 71 (Karachentsev Kaisin ; Boyce et al. ) based on HI detections and H emission. Also among the dwarfs that coincide with the LG dSph locus, one dwarf is classified as transition-type, namely HS 117, with HI associated with it (Huchtmeier Skillman ; Karachentsev et al. ).

The most distant dSph is DDO 44 which belongs to the NGC 2403 subgroup, while KDG 61 is the one closest to the M 81 itself. Interestingely, the dwarf KDG 61 which is classified as a transition-type based on HI detections, is the closest to the M 81 itself, with a deprojected distance of 44 kpc (Karachentsev et al. ).

All of them show either a spatial variation of the centroids of the two stellar populations, as is the case of F12D1 and KDG 64, or that the metal-rich population is more centrally concentrated, as is the case of DDO 71, DDO 44 and F6D1. These findings agree well with the ones from the cumulative histograms, though we should keep in mind that the metal-poor and metal-rich stellar populations involved are differently selected. DDO 78 and IKN are clearly fairly metal-rich while KDG 61, KDG 64 and DDO 44 have prominent metal-poor populations.

sources.summary: The paper treats KDG 61 as a dwarf spheroidal screen: its nearby HII region has velocities matching M 81 outskirts and is likely projected, not associated with KDG 61 itself.

quotes.quote: Radial velocities of the clumps measured by us, as well as the velocity of HII-region in the dSph galaxy KDG 61 are compatible with their location at the outskirts of a large rotating gaseous disc around M 81.

a dwarf spheroidal galaxy KDG 61 is present. On its NE side a bright HII region is visible, for which jon97 have measured the radial velocity V_h=-135 30 km s ^ 1 . Later, similar values of radial velocity for this emission spot: V_h=-116 21 km s ^ 1 and 123 6 km s ^ 1 were obtained by sha01 and mak10 . Since mak10 measured the radial velocity of +222 3 km s ^ 1 for the central globular cluster in KDG 61, they assumed that the bright HII region is not associated with the galaxy KDG 61 itself, but that it is rather projected onto it from the far periphery of the M 81.

sources.summary: The paper discusses KDG 61 as a transition-galaxy case complicated by confusion from M 81 and reports nearby HI clouds not related to KDG 61 itself.

quotes.quote: KDG 61 claim that this is a clear case of a transition galaxy. Their beam, however, is 12^ large and they are limited by confusion due to HI coming from the triplet. KDG 61 lies close to M 81 and, even with our much higher resolution data, it is not trivial to separate emission from KDG 61 or other field objects from M 81 emission. We seem to find several HI clouds which are neither related to the SE tidal arm nor to KDG 61 itself, at velocities ranging from 45 to 15 km s ^ 1 .

gmw_00008330.name: BK5N
gmw_00008330.overview: BK5N is a dwarf spheroidal galaxy located in the M81 group [arXiv:astro-ph/0507168]. Its stellar population includes upper-asymptotic giant branch stars, which indicates that star formation in the galaxy continued until approximately 8 billion years ago [arXiv:astro-ph/0507168].

sources.summary: The paper finds HI clouds offset from BK5N, considers expelled material from BK5N and KDG 64, and disfavors that origin because the clouds are spatially uncorrelated.

quotes.quote: BK5N Several HI clouds are detected with velocities from 130 to 75 km s ^ 1 , well offset from the optical counterpart (for which no optical radial velocity has been published).

They could be: primordial material which is infalling towards the M 81 triplet; High Velocity Cloud (HVC) analogues belonging to M 81; material originally part of BK5N and KDG 64 which has been expelled; or tidal debris scattered into the SE quadrant.

The third option, that they represent material that has been expelled from BK5N and KDG 64 is also hard to defend. Not only do the clouds seem uncorrelated spatially with both dwarfs, but in the one case where we have velocity information for the dwarf, the velocities of the clouds are offset substantially.

sources.summary: The paper describes BK 5N as an M81-group dwarf spheroidal with upper-AGB stars, indicating ongoing star formation until about 8 Gyr ago.

quotes.quote: The first investigation of the stellar populations of dSph galaxies in this group was that of , who used multi-orbit HST/WFPC2 V and I imaging to determine -m diagrams for two M81 group dSphs, F8D1 and BK 5N . The latter system has M _ V -11.3 and lies approximately 70kpc from M81 and 30kpc from NGC 3077 (in projection). The m diagram clearly shows that an upper-AGB population is present in this galaxy, indicating on-going star formation. estimate the upper-AGB termination luminosity as M _ bol -4.3, which suggests that stars formed in this dSph until 8 Gyr ago.

gmw_00008331.name: KDG 64
gmw_00008331.overview: KDG 64 is a transition-type dwarf spheroidal galaxy located in the M81 group [arXiv:1003.0861, arXiv:2206.07059]. It features an old, metal-poor stellar population with no young stars [arXiv:2206.07059], a spatial variation in the centroids of its stellar populations [arXiv:1003.0861], and offset neutral hydrogen emission [arXiv:0708.2836].

sources.summary: KDG 64 is described as a transition-type M81-group dwarf in the dSph/dIrr border region, with centroid shifts between stellar populations and a prominent metal-poor population.

quotes.quote: The M 81 group dSphs that seem to lie in this apparent transition region are KDG 61, KDG 64, DDO 44 and DDO 71.
