NGC 4565 is clearly interacting with IC 3571, as can be seen in Figure fig1 by the bridge of material between them. Additionally, material closer to the plane of the disk appears to be displaced above the plane, toward IC 3571 (Figure fig1 ). Given the location of IC 3571, it may contribute to the asymmetry in the component of the warp across the line of sight.

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 3571 as a northern companion of 4565 with H I mass, evidence of interaction, and a weak bridge with a smooth velocity change between the systems.

quotes.quote: To the north is IC 3571 M_ H I =4.18 10^7 ; hea11 , which shows evidence of interaction with 4565. Specifically, a weak bridge is found between the disk of 4565 and this companion galaxy, which exhibits a smooth change in velocity between the two systems van05,hea11 . The initial extension of this bridge from 4565 can be seen as an extended shelf in Figure fig:ngc4565 at (R.A., decl.) ( 12^ h 36^ m 15^ s , 26 03 ).

gmw_00008566.name: NVSS J172216+320910
gmw_00008566.overview: NVSS J172216+320910 is a probable Fanaroff-Riley type I radio galaxy located northwest of the cluster Abell 2261 [arXiv:1306.3102]. It features a compact central core and filamentary diffuse emission extending on two sides [arXiv:1306.3102].

sources.summary: The paper describes NVSS J172216+320910 as a large probable FR-I radio galaxy northwest of A2261, with a compact core, filamentary diffuse emission, and measured radio fluxes.

quotes.quote: A remarkable radio galaxy is seen ( 2.5' = 540 kpc) toward the northwest of the cluster center. The radio galaxy shows presence of a compact source at the center and diffuse emission extended on two sides of it (Fig. a2261opt ). The diffuse emission is filamentary and of total extent 590 kpc (if at the redshift of the cluster). Based on the morphology it is possibly an `FR-I' type radio galaxy. The total flux density (core + diffuse) of the radio galaxy at 610 MHz is 57 6 mJy and 23.3 mJy at 1.4 GHz (NVSS J172216+320910).

gmw_00008567.name: NGC1633
gmw_00008567.overview: NGC1633 is a spiral galaxy located at a redshift of 0.016642 [arXiv:1306.3102]. It exhibits diffuse radio emission with a linear size of 16 kpc, which is likely synchrotron emission originating from the galaxy's disk [arXiv:1306.3102].

sources.summary: The paper reports diffuse radio emission coincident with spiral galaxy NGC1633, interpreting it as likely synchrotron emission from the galaxy disk with a 16 kpc extent.

quotes.quote: Overlay of the radio image on the Digitized Sky Survey R-band image revealed the presence of the galaxy NGC1633 at location of the diffuse patch (Fig. 7rx , left). NGC1633 is a spiral galaxy at a redshift of 0.016642 (De Vaucouleurs et al. 1991, RC3.9). Since there is no compact source detected in higher resolution images made at 610 and 235 MHz, this diffuse emission is most likely synchrotron emission from the disk of the galaxy. The largest linear size of the emission detected at 610 MHz is 16 kpc. The flux densities at 610 and 235 MHz estimated using primary beam corrected images are 13.4 2.0 mJy and 29.0 4.0 mJy, respectively.

gmw_00008568.name: NVSSJ043652+071420
gmw_00008568.overview: NVSSJ043652+071420 is a radio galaxy with a compact core and jets oriented in a northeast-southwest direction [arXiv:1306.3102]. The core exhibits self-absorbed flux densities, and the galaxy is associated with two candidate diffuse radio lobes detected along the jet axis [arXiv:1306.3102].

sources.summary: The paper resolves NVSSJ043652+071420 into a radio galaxy with northeast-southwest jets, a compact core, self-absorbed core fluxes, and candidate diffuse lobes.

quotes.quote: The NVSS source NVSSJ043652+071420, is resolved at 610 and 235 MHz to reveal a radio galaxy with jets oriented in the direction northeast -- southwest (Fig. 7rx , right). The core is coincident with a galaxy detected in the UKIRT Infra Red Survey (Fig. 7rx , right). The flux densities at 610 and 235 MHz of the core region are 78 7 mJy and 69 5 mJy, respectively, indicating a self-absorption. Also along the same northeast -- southwest direction, two diffuse sources are detected in the 235 MHz image. We propose that these are `lobes' associated with the radio galaxy.

gmw_00008569.name: UGC 8215
gmw_00008569.overview: UGC 8215 is a small dwarf irregular galaxy characterized by a relatively steep light profile [arXiv:astro-ph/9903348]. Its central region exhibits a slight deficit compared to a pure exponential profile, and it displays a flat or slightly bluing color profile toward larger radii [arXiv:astro-ph/9903348].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 8215 as a small irregular with a steep light profile, central exponential deficit, and slight outward bluing or flat color profile.

quotes.quote: UGC 8215 : small irregular with a relatively steep light profile. The central part shows a slight deficit compared to a pure exponential. One of the few galaxies with a slight bluing of its colours towards larger radii (but could also have a flat colour profile within the errors).

gmw_00008570.name: UGCA 342
gmw_00008570.overview: UGCA 342 is a very irregular dwarf galaxy projected close to a bright star [arXiv:astro-ph/9903348]. Located only 7.7 arcminutes from M63, its velocity and diffuse features suggest it may be a luminous condensation in the outer part of M63 [arXiv:astro-ph/9903348].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGCA 342 as irregular, near a bright star and M63, with velocity and diffuse features suggesting a luminous condensation in M63's outer part.

quotes.quote: UGCA 342 : very irregular, and projected close to a bright star. At a velocity of 388 km s ^ 1 it might belong to the outskirts of M 63, which has a redshift of 504 km s ^ 1 . Indeed, UGCA 342 is only 7.7' from M 63, which is less than one optical diameter of M 63. At a distance of 8.5 Mpc ( H_0 60 ) this represents 19 kpc in projected distance. Some diffuse features are visible in the image, especially to the south-east of UGCA 342, supporting the idea that UGCA 342 is some luminous condensation in the outer part of M 63.

gmw_00008571.name: DDO 169
gmw_00008571.overview: DDO 169 is a dwarf galaxy that exhibits surface brightness and color profile behaviors similar to those of DDO 168 [arXiv:astro-ph/9903348]. The galaxy features an extended tail toward the north that is slightly bluer than the rest of its body [arXiv:astro-ph/9903348].

sources.summary: The paper says DDO 169 resembles DDO 168 in surface-brightness and color-profile behavior and has an extended northward tail that is slightly bluer.

quotes.quote: DDO 169 : like DDO 168, one observes similar features in the surface brightness and colour profiles. Extended `tail' towards the north. This tail seems to be slightly bluer than the rest of the galaxy.

gmw_00008572.name: NGC 5229
gmw_00008572.overview: NGC 5229 is an almost edge-on galaxy with an elongated shape [arXiv:astro-ph/9903348]. The galaxy also exhibits a slightly warped geometry [arXiv:astro-ph/9903348].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5229 as an almost edge-on, slightly warped and elongated galaxy whose geometry makes circular-aperture photometry inadequate.

quotes.quote: NGC 5229 also shows an excess in one colour but in this case our photometric method is clearly not well suited, due to the galaxy being almost edge-on and warped.
NGC 5229 : edge on, slightly warped.
Elongated galaxies, as illustrated by NGC 5229, which has an apparent ellipticity of =0.81 , show different effective radii as measured by the different methods.

gmw_00008573.name: UGC 08882
gmw_00008573.overview: UGC 08882 is a nucleated dwarf elliptical galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9903348]. The galaxy features a visible inner luminosity cusp, a flat color profile, and a nucleus that is slightly blue relative to the rest of the system [arXiv:astro-ph/9903348].

sources.summary: The paper presents UGC 08882/UGC 8882 as the sample's lone dwarf elliptical, with a visible inner cusp, flat color profile, and slightly blue nucleus.

quotes.quote: With one exception (the dwarf elliptical UGC 8882) they are all late-type dwarfs (Sd, Sm, Im).

The inner deviation from the exponential can be a luminosity cusp, which is common among dwarf ellipticals, especially nucleated ones. This feature is well seen in the only dwarf elliptical of our sample, UGC 8882.
UGC 08882 : nucleated dwarf elliptical with a flat colour profile and a slightly blue nucleus relative to the bulk of the galaxy.

gmw_00008574.name: MGC 9-23-21
gmw_00008574.overview: MGC 9-23-21 is identified as a possible blue compact dwarf galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9903348]. It is projected very close to a bright star, which creates a large light gradient that makes accurate photometry of its fainter parts difficult [arXiv:astro-ph/9903348].

sources.summary: The paper identifies MGC 9-23-21 as a possible blue compact dwarf member but says a nearby bright star and light gradient make faint photometry difficult.

quotes.quote: We have therefore made an attempt to find new candidate members on deep POSS II Schmidt films, but found only one additional possible member (the BCD MGC 9-23-21).

MGC 9-23-21 : projected very close to a bright star, making accurate photometry very difficult. The relatively large light gradient around the galaxy more or less excludes accurate photometry of the fainter parts, especially considering the small angular size.

gmw_00008575.overview: The galaxy 2034 is a nearly face-on dwarf galaxy [arXiv:0901.4222]. It exhibits a small kinematic warp, with its position angle changing by approximately 35 degrees [arXiv:0901.4222].

sources.summary: The paper describes 2034 as a nearly face-on dwarf with a small kinematic warp, a changing position angle, and uncertain radial inclination variation.

quotes.quote: 2034 has a small kinematic warp. The position angle changes by about 35^ . Because the galaxy is almost face-on, the variation of inclination angle with radius is difficult to determine.

gmw_00008576.overview: The dwarf galaxy 2053 has its kinematic major axis and its morphological major axis oriented almost at right angles [arXiv:0901.4222]. Its kinematics are reminiscent of a bar potential, and while its bright optical image has a bar-like shape, no faint disk is detected in optical or neutral hydrogen data [arXiv:0901.4222].

sources.summary: The paper says 2053 has nearly perpendicular kinematic and morphological axes, bar-like kinematics, and no faint disk detected in optical or H I data.

quotes.quote: 2053 has its kinematic major axis and its morphological major axis almost at right angles. Its kinematics are reminiscent of that expected for a bar potential. The bright optical image appears to have a bar-like shape, but no faint disk is detected, neither in the optical nor in the .

gmw_00008577.overview: The object 3137 is an edge-on dwarf galaxy [arXiv:0901.4222]. Due to its high inclination and a rotation curve that rises steeply relative to the beam size, its H I rotation curve is difficult to determine [arXiv:0901.4222].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3137 as an edge-on galaxy whose steeply rising curve and inclination make its H I rotation curve difficult to determine.

quotes.quote: 3137 . The \ rotation curve rises more steeply than the \ one. This galaxy is edge-on, and has a rotation curve that rises steeply with respect to the size of the \ beam. This, in combination with its inclination, makes the \ rotation curve hard to determine.

gmw_00008578.name: Cloud 1
gmw_00008578.overview: Cloud 1 is a low surface brightness galaxy located midway between the giant spiral galaxies M95 and M96 [arXiv:2209.10994]. It is situated at a similar velocity to these two spirals, but it exhibits an asymmetric spectral profile that suggests the presence of multiple kinematic components [arXiv:2209.10994].

sources.summary: Cloud 1 lies between M95 and M96 but is offset in position-velocity space, has a higher velocity width, and may contain multiple kinematic components.

quotes.quote: Clouds 1-4 are midway between the giant spiral galaxies M95 and M96, which have a projected separation of approximately 0.7 degrees, equivalent to 136 kpc. The clouds are also at similar velocities to the two spirals (M95 is at 779 , M96 is at 888 ), as shown by the PV-diagram in figure fig:pvclouds . Cloud 1 is however somewhat deviant from the trend in P-V space seen from clouds 1-4.

Intriguingly, the W20 measurement of cloud 1 appears to be accurate - indeed, if anything it seems more likely that the W50 is an underestimate. We note that, unlike the other clouds, its spectral profile is clearly asymmetric (figure fig:cloudset1 ), suggesting that it might have multiple components and reinforcing the need for higher resolution observations.

gmw_00008579.name: Cloud 2
gmw_00008579.overview: Cloud 2 is an H I region located between the galaxies M95 and M96 [arXiv:2209.10994]. While it does not belong to a larger H I bridge, smoothed data reveals that Cloud 2 has a tentative connection to the disc of M96 [arXiv:2209.10994].

sources.summary: The paper places Cloud 2 between M95 and M96, finds a tentative smoothed-data connection to M96, but sees no larger H I bridge linking the spirals.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, this extra smoothing does not alter the appearance of M96, M95 or cloud 1, but the other clouds do appear more extended. Cloud 2 shows a tentative connection to M96. Somewhat surprisingly, cloud 4 appears to be marginally resolved, spanning approximately two beams - given that it has an optical counterpart, we would expect it to have a higher column density than the other features and so be more compact, yet this is not the case. However, this is a further indication that our size estimates in section sec:size are unlikely to be wide of the mark. Cloud 3 becomes significantly more extended, spanning four beams (45 kpc) with a clear velocity gradient across the whole feature (the velocity difference between the most extreme contours where the cloud is visible is 67 ), though it does not appear to directly connect with the disc of M96. The extra Hanning smoothing also reveals a tentative additional detection here labelled as cloud 7. In the standard cube this is just visible but appears as an extension of cloud 2.

While cloud 2 does resemble some of the clouds discussed in (where features which appeared as discrete at a lower sensitivity were revealed as being connected to the disc of M33 at a higher sensitivity), the smoothing does not suggest the disc itself is likely to be much larger than it appears in the standard cube. This does not rule out a tidal origin for the clouds, but does not fit the general expectation of tidal stripping.

gmw_00008580.name: Cloud 3
gmw_00008580.overview: Cloud 3 is a marginally resolved and extended neutral hydrogen source with a diameter of approximately 20 kpc [arXiv:2209.10994]. If the object is stable, it would be extremely dark-matter dominated, with a dynamical-to-gas mass ratio of at least 140 [arXiv:2209.10994].

sources.summary: Cloud 3 is marginally resolved and extended, may span about 20 kpc or more after smoothing, and would be extremely dark-matter dominated if stable.

quotes.quote: Cloud 3 is marginally resolved, with the 3.5 contours spanning approximately two beams. This gives a diameter of 20 kpc. Although the other clouds are not resolved, the large size of this cloud and its similar mass tentatively suggest that the others are unlikely to be much smaller than the beam size. At a typical mass of 5.0 10 ^ 6 , a source filling the 11.3 kpc diameter beam would have a column density of 0.05 pc ^ 2 (6.3 10 ^ 18 cm ^ 2 ). Conversely, to have a column density of the more typical 6 pc ^ 2 found in dwarf galaxies ( leroy ), the diameter would be around 1 kpc. Such a column density is technically possible, as the circular-average column density may be misleading : since even cloud 3 is only marginally resolved, it is possible that it is much smaller along one axis.

For cloud 3 we may take the radius to be 11 kpc since it is marginally resolved. With a rotation speed v_ circ of W50/2 = 21 (note that though the line width is small, it does appear to show a velocity gradient - though we lack sufficient resolution to say if this is the result of ordered motions), this gives M _ dyn 1 10 ^ 9 (this is a lower limit as we do not correct for inclination), giving a ratio M _ dyn /M 140. This would be an extremely dark matter-dominated object.

gmw_00008581.name: LeG 13
gmw_00008581.overview: LeG 13 is a dwarf galaxy that is identified with a specific gas cloud, designated as cloud 4 [arXiv:2209.10994]. It is the only cloud in its immediate group that possesses a clear optical counterpart [arXiv:2209.10994].

sources.summary: LeG 13 is identified with cloud 4, the only cloud with a clear optical counterpart, closely matched to the H I position and similar to the dark clouds.

quotes.quote: Only cloud 4 has a clear detection at optical wavelengths : the galaxy LeG 13 (AGC 202024). We regard the optical counterpart as being almost certainly associated with the emission even though there is no corroborating optical redshift measurement, as the optical and coordinates differ by just 13 (with no other similar objects nearby). Furthermore the optical component is sufficiently resolved that it appears more likely to be a group member than a more distant background object. There are no instances in the rest of the AGES sample of finding a single optical counterpart candidate so close to the coordinates in which, where available, the optical redshift determination did not closely match the measurement. The possibility of a coincidental alignment of the optical and components can therefore be neglected.

Very near to these three clouds, and at a similar velocity, the dwarf galaxy LeG 13 (cloud 4 in our catalogue) is detected with similar properties to the dark clouds but with a clear optical counterpart. Elsewhere in the group, another object (cloud 6) is seen with no apparent association to the Ring or any of the galaxies present. None of its nearest detected neighbours show any signs of disturbances in their gas discs. With the nearby galaxies having masses two to four orders of magnitude greater than the clouds, which are themselves readily detected, even a small perturbation to their ought to be easily detectable with AGES -- and their distances are anyway well outside the Leo Group. This makes the parent galaxy of cloud 6 extremely challenging to identify. All of the clouds (including LeG 13) possess comparable masses and velocity widths, but in other aspects the clouds are dissimilar. While clouds 1-4 are all between M95 and M96, cloud 1 is offset in velocity from the others and has a higher velocity width. Cloud 2 may be connected to the disc of M96, while cloud 3 appears significantly extended when spectral smoothing is applied. Only cloud 4, LeG 13, shows an optical counterpart.

gmw_00008582.name: Cloud 5
gmw_00008582.overview: Cloud 5 is an H I region located in close proximity to the Leo Ring [arXiv:2209.10994]. Although it was likely produced by the same processes that formed the Ring, its narrow line width suggests it is currently detached from the larger structure [arXiv:2209.10994].

sources.summary: Cloud 5 sits near the Leo Ring but appears detached, lacks extra smoothed emission, has a narrow line width, and was probably produced with the Ring.

quotes.quote: While it does appear distinct, cloud 5 is in close proximity to the Leo Ring. This region of the Ring is highly disorganised, and it is possible this is an outlying cloud formed by the same processes that produced the rest of the large-scale emission in this region. To the north, a much larger and more massive cloud is also detached but appears to be part of the Ring. Whereas the northern feature clearly fits neatly into the ellipsoid structure of the rest of the , cloud 5 is not aligned with any other features. Moreover, the additional smoothing described for clouds 1 - 4 does not reveal any additional component to cloud 5, and its narrower line width suggests that it is - at least at present - truly detached from the Ring and not an outlying part of the larger object. Still, the most obvious explanation is that cloud 5 was produced along with the rest of the Ring, a process we will explore in a future work.

gmw_00008583.name: Cloud 6
gmw_00008583.overview: Cloud 6 is a compact gas cloud that is isolated from the Leo Ring [arXiv:2209.10994]. Its distance and kinematics make both a ring-related origin and a tidal-parent explanation difficult to reconcile [arXiv:2209.10994].

sources.summary: Cloud 6 is isolated from the Leo Ring and nearby galaxies; its distances and kinematics make both ring-related and tidal-parent explanations difficult.

quotes.quote: Cloud 6 is much more intriguing. While it is possible that it too was formed by the same mechanism that created the ring (e.g. the collisional origin proposed by leocollide ), its distances and kinematics argue against this interpretation. It is 0.65 degrees away from the nearest point on the Ring, about 125 kpc in projection, and does not align with any features in the Ring. The distance to M105, at the approximate centre of the Ring, is about 1.1 degrees, 210 kpc. At the estimated evolution time of 250 Myr (section sec:expand ), this would require a projected velocity of 820 .

Almost all of this would have to be across the plane of the sky, since the velocity of the cloud falls well within the velocity of material of the Ring. In contrast, the Ring itself only spans about 400 . The kinematics of the cloud and the Ring thus do not match : it is very difficult to see what sort of process could produce a gigantic, coherent arc of material and also one single, compact cloud that was ejected at least at twice the velocity of everything else.

gmw_00008584.name: NGC 3338
gmw_00008584.overview: NGC 3338 is a massive spiral galaxy [arXiv:2209.10994]. It is an undisturbed background galaxy located at a redshift-independent distance of approximately 22.6 Mpc [arXiv:2209.10994].

sources.summary: The paper considers NGC 3338 as a possible Cloud 6 parent, but rejects association because it is farther away, velocity-separated, undisturbed, and likely background.

quotes.quote: Near to this pair of galaxies is a third, much more massive spiral NGC 3338 (note that while the pair are not visible in figure fig:wholeleo as they are outside the velocity range shown, part of NGC 3338 is just visible). This is rather further away at 0.77 degrees (147 kpc) and even more separated in velocity (440 ) from the cloud. While the spiral/irregular pair do show disturbances in their optical morphology, none of these three galaxies show significantly extended emission : we can easily distinguish the in the pair from the giant spiral, and there is no hint of any extension towards cloud 6. In addition, NED gives a mean redshift-independent distance estimate to NGC 3338 of 22.6 Mpc, twice the distance to the M96 subgroup. Likewise, the fact that all of the clouds are found within a velocity range of 100 while UGC 05832 and CGC 065-090 are found at a difference of 360 argues against their association with any of the clouds. Again, given the low velocity dispersion of the Leo group, it seems much more probable that all the clouds are found at this much lower distance and are unrelated to any of the background galaxies.

gmw_00008585.name: SDSS J121804.26+144510.4
gmw_00008585.overview: SDSS J121804.26+144510.4 is a nearby dwarf galaxy of unclear nature that is modeled as a dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:0710.3867]. It has an observed neutral hydrogen mass of 20 million solar masses and may be physically interacting with VirgoHI21 [arXiv:0710.3867].

sources.summary: SDSS J121804.26+144510.4 is described as a nearby dwarf of unclear nature whose possible interaction could locally disturb VirgoHI21's velocity field and is modeled as a dwarf spheroid.

quotes.quote: Alternatively, the velocity field can be disturbed by objects in the neighborhood, for instance by the nearby dwarf galaxy, SDSS J121804.26+144510.4, also known as object C (see Fig. fig:vhi21 and M07). The nature of this dwarf is actually unclear (see Appendix). We suppose here that it is a pre-existing object, physically interacting with the system. Object C has a visible HI mass of 2 10^7 M _ .

We included its possible influence in a simple model. We describe it as a dwarf spheroid with a Plummer profile, total mass of 4 10^8 M _ (to include the dark matter mass) and scale-length of 5 kpc. It is located 8 kpc East from VirgoHI21 on the sky plane, and we assume it lies 20 kpc below VirgoHI21 in the radial direction.

gmw_00008586.name: object 35
gmw_00008586.overview: Object 35 is a compact radio source with an optical appearance and spectral energy distribution that suggest it is a quasar [arXiv:1405.1711]. Although it lacks spectroscopic information, modeling of its spectral energy distribution and colors indicates a redshift of approximately 1.8 [arXiv:1405.1711].

sources.summary: The paper treats object 35 as a compact-radio QSO candidate: its optical appearance and SED suggest a quasar, SDSS colors favor z=1.8, and the authors reclassify it as QSO.

quotes.quote: Unfortunately, the third object (namely, 35) does not have any available spectroscopic information. It is associated with a compact radio source.
We also model the SED of object 35, although it is optical appearance and its SED shape suggests an identification as a QSO.

For object 35 the ^ 2 minimum indicates a redshift of 1.8, different from that we derive from the SED modeling (1.03). We then finally change the classification of this source to QSO because of its SED and optical appearance and use as redshift z=1.80^ +0.40 _ 0.40 .

gmw_00008587.name: NGC 5778
gmw_00008587.overview: NGC 5778 is the central dominant brightest cluster galaxy of Abell 1991 [arXiv:1301.2928, arXiv:astro-ph/0405336]. It hosts a compact, low-power radio source [arXiv:astro-ph/0405336] and exhibits modest star formation with little accreted gas [arXiv:astro-ph/0405336].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5778 as Abell 1991's central dominant galaxy, offset from the X-ray peak and coincident with a radio source associated with the southern cavity.

quotes.quote: This figure reveals the bright X-ray region with its peak emission centroid located at (RA = 14:54:31.7) and (DEC = +18:38:42), showing an offset of 10 relative to that in optical/radio image (RA = 14:54:31.5; DEC= +18:38:32) centered on NGC 5778 1999ApJS..125...35S . The BCG appears to coincide with the S-cavity and shows an off-set of 10 relative to the X-ray peak. As S-cavity has associated radio source, therefore, is defined as ``clear cavity", while N-cavity failing to show such an association, can be defined as ``ghost cavity" 2001ApJ...562L.149M .

This figure confirms the coincidence of a point-like source with the central dominant galaxy NGC 5778 and is consistent with that reported by 2004ApJ...613..180S , however, shows an offset of 10 relative to the X-ray peak of the cluster. Though this radio source shows an association with the S-cavity, no such association is evident for the case of N-cavity.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5778 as Abell 1991’s brightest central galaxy, hosting a compact low-power radio source with modest star formation, little accreted gas, and an offset X-ray core.

quotes.quote: A1991 purportedly hosts a moderate cooling flow of M 115 (Stewart et al. 1984), and its brightest central galaxy (BCG), NGC 5778, hosts a compact, relatively low-power radio source with log \ P_ 1400 \ MHz = 23.41 \ W \ Hz^ 1 (Owen Ledlow 1997). Only modest levels of star formation (McNamara O'Connell 1992) and accreted gas ( M_ HI < 3.6 10^9 ; McNamara et al. 1990) are in evidence in NGC 5778, implying that the cooling flow is largely in remission.

The X--ray centroid (J2000 RA, Dec=14:54:31.7, +18:38:35) and peak (J2000 RA, Dec=14:54:31.5, +18:38:42), determined over a roughly 100 arcsec central region of the X--ray image, are offset by a few arcsec from the optical/radio position (J2000 RA, Dec=14:54:31.5, +18:38:32.0) of the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG), NGC 5778. At about 10 arcsec north of the X--ray centroid, there is enhanced emission (shown in the lower panel of Fig. fig:clusimage1 and discussed in sec:knotmorph below).

gmw_00008588.name: 2MASX J16182316+5527217
gmw_00008588.overview: The galaxy 2MASX J16182316+5527217 is classified as a starburst galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.1 [arXiv:1210.1644]. It exhibits clear and strong polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon emission at 3.3 micrometers [arXiv:1210.1644].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MASX J16182316+5527217 as one of two starburst-SED AMUSES galaxies with clear, strong 3.3 micron PAH emission and gives their average PAH luminosity.

quotes.quote: Detection and strength of the 3.3 m PAH emission We detect the 3.3 m PAH feature from three galaxies out of 20 target galaxies. Although the 3.3 m PAH features are generally pretty weak across the sample, a couple galaxies of starburst SED class, namely 2MASX J16182316+5527217 and 2MASX J16205879+5425127 show very clear and strong 3.3 m PAH features. While these two galaxies have starburst SEDs, the remaining one galaxy, 2MASX J10542172+5823445 has an AGN SED.
The two starburst galaxies with 3.3 PAH detection are at z 0.1 and their average luminosity is 9.46 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 .

gmw_00008589.name: 2MASX J16205879+5425127
gmw_00008589.overview: 2MASX J16205879+5425127 is a starburst galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.063 [arXiv:1210.1644]. It exhibits a starburst spectral energy distribution and displays clear 3.3 micron polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon emission [arXiv:1210.1644].

sources.summary: The paper reports 2MASX J16205879+5425127 as a starburst-SED galaxy with clear 3.3 micron PAH emission, z=0.063, and the lowest PAH luminosity among the detections.

quotes.quote: Detection and strength of the 3.3 m PAH emission We detect the 3.3 m PAH feature from three galaxies out of 20 target galaxies. Although the 3.3 m PAH features are generally pretty weak across the sample, a couple galaxies of starburst SED class, namely 2MASX J16182316+5527217 and 2MASX J16205879+5425127 show very clear and strong 3.3 m PAH features. While these two galaxies have starburst SEDs, the remaining one galaxy, 2MASX J10542172+5823445 has an AGN SED.

The 3.3 PAH luminosities range from 7.11 10 ^ 42 erg s ^ 1 of an AGN at z = 0.205 (2MASX J10542172+5823445) to 7.14 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 of a starburst galaxy at z =0.063 (2MASX J16205879+5425127). The two starburst galaxies with 3.3 PAH detection are at z 0.1 and their average luminosity is 9.46 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 .

gmw_00008590.name: 2MASX J10542172+5823445
gmw_00008590.overview: 2MASX J10542172+5823445 is an active galactic nucleus host galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.205 [arXiv:1210.1644]. It exhibits a 3.3 micron polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon emission feature with a luminosity of 7.11 x 10^42 erg/s [arXiv:1210.1644].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MASX J10542172+5823445 as the AGN-SED AMUSES galaxy with detected 3.3 micron PAH emission and the highest PAH luminosity in the detected range.

quotes.quote: Detection and strength of the 3.3 m PAH emission We detect the 3.3 m PAH feature from three galaxies out of 20 target galaxies. Although the 3.3 m PAH features are generally pretty weak across the sample, a couple galaxies of starburst SED class, namely 2MASX J16182316+5527217 and 2MASX J16205879+5425127 show very clear and strong 3.3 m PAH features. While these two galaxies have starburst SEDs, the remaining one galaxy, 2MASX J10542172+5823445 has an AGN SED.
The 3.3 PAH luminosities range from 7.11 10 ^ 42 erg s ^ 1 of an AGN at z = 0.205 (2MASX J10542172+5823445) to 7.14 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 of a starburst galaxy at z =0.063 (2MASX J16205879+5425127).

gmw_00008591.name: MATLAS-1824
gmw_00008591.overview: MATLAS-1824 is an ultra-diffuse dwarf galaxy detected in neutral hydrogen [arXiv:2111.14491]. It is a satellite of an early-type galaxy and is a dark-matter-deficient candidate, which supports a tidal origin [arXiv:2111.14491].

sources.summary: The paper describes MATLAS-1824 as an HI-detected UDG and dark-matter-deficient tidal dwarf candidate, with low dynamical-to-baryonic ratio and satellite status supporting a tidal origin.

quotes.quote: Of the 59 UDGs in the MATLAS dwarf sample, 51 are located in the regions observed by the ALFALFA and ATLAS ^ 3D HI surveys and three have an HI line detection (MATLAS-42, MATLAS-1337 and MATLAS-1824). These galaxies are located at distances from 33 to 46.5 Mpc with HI masses 8.1 log(M _ HI ) 8.3 , line widths 24 < W _ 50 36 km s ^ 1 , and (g-)_0 color in the range 0.06 -- 0.4. They have M _ HI , W _ 50 and (g-)_0 values in the range of those observed for HI-bearing UDGs in other studies (e.g., Leisman2017, Kunakaran2020 ).
We note that these two UDGs are dark matter deficient candidates with a dynamical-to-baryonic mass ratio of 2.8 and 2.9 for MATLAS-1824 and MATLAS-42, respectively.

Only one HI-bearing UDG (MATLAS-1824) is satellite of an ETG from this subsample, the other two being likely located in groups. This UDG is relatively close to its host and show a very low dynamical-to-mass ratio, in agreement with its tidal origin (see Section section:TDGs .) Local density We examine possible effects of the local density on the gas content and the morphology of the HI-bearing dwarfs.

gmw_00008592.name: MATLAS-42
gmw_00008592.overview: MATLAS-42 is an HI-detected ultra-diffuse galaxy [arXiv:2111.14491]. Based on its dynamical-to-baryonic mass ratio of 2.9, it is considered a candidate for being dark matter deficient [arXiv:2111.14491].

sources.summary: The paper describes MATLAS-42 as an HI-detected ultra-diffuse galaxy whose HI mass, line width, color, and dynamical-to-baryonic ratio make it a dark-matter-deficient candidate.

quotes.quote: This represent a sample of five traditional dwarfs (MATLAS-146, MATLAS-580, MATLAS-1194, MATLAS-1750 and MATLAS-2126) and two UDGs (MATLAS-42 and MATLAS-1824).

Of the 59 UDGs in the MATLAS dwarf sample, 51 are located in the regions observed by the ALFALFA and ATLAS ^ 3D HI surveys and three have an HI line detection (MATLAS-42, MATLAS-1337 and MATLAS-1824). These galaxies are located at distances from 33 to 46.5 Mpc with HI masses 8.1 log(M _ HI ) 8.3 , line widths 24 < W _ 50 36 km s ^ 1 , and (g-)_0 color in the range 0.06 -- 0.4. They have M _ HI , W _ 50 and (g-)_0 values in the range of those observed for HI-bearing UDGs in other studies (e.g., Leisman2017, Kunakaran2020 ).
We note that these two UDGs are dark matter deficient candidates with a dynamical-to-baryonic mass ratio of 2.8 and 2.9 for MATLAS-1824 and MATLAS-42, respectively.

gmw_00008593.name: MATLAS-1830
gmw_00008593.overview: MATLAS-1830 is a gas-rich tidal dwarf galaxy candidate with a detected neutral hydrogen line [arXiv:2111.14491]. It exhibits a very low surface brightness, which has prevented successful two-dimensional surface brightness modeling [arXiv:2111.14491].

sources.summary: The paper treats MATLAS-1830 as a gas-rich tidal dwarf candidate with an HI line detection, also noting its UDG status is unresolved because surface-brightness modeling failed.

quotes.quote: Three of these are tidal dwarf candidates (MATLAS-1824, MATLAS-1322 and MATLAS-1830; see Section section:interactions ), explaining their high gas content that might originate from massive galaxies.
Among these dwarfs, four have an HI line detection in our study (MATLAS-785, MATLAS-1750, MATLAS-1824 and MATLAS-1830).

However, we note that one of these UDGs (MATLAS-1830) is not in the subsample of 59 UDGs considered in this work due to the absence of successful 2D surface brightness modeling caused by its very low surface brightness.

gmw_00008594.name: MATLAS-1750
gmw_00008594.overview: MATLAS-1750 is a dark-matter-deficient dwarf galaxy candidate that exhibits an elliptical shape [arXiv:2111.14491]. The galaxy contains internal structures and has been detected in neutral hydrogen emission [arXiv:2111.14491].

sources.summary: The paper identifies MATLAS-1750 as a traditional dark-matter-deficient dwarf candidate with internal structure and as a previously identified tidal dwarf candidate with HI detection.

quotes.quote: This represent a sample of five traditional dwarfs (MATLAS-146, MATLAS-580, MATLAS-1194, MATLAS-1750 and MATLAS-2126) and two UDGs (MATLAS-42 and MATLAS-1824).

For example, 20 dwarfs identified as dIs have 0.5 < M_ HI /L_B < 1 . These dwarfs all show an elliptical shape as well as internal structures (e.g. MATLAS-1750, MATLAS-519), large star-forming regions or signs of dwarf-dwarf interactions (e.g. the shells of MATLAS-602 discussed in Section section:interactions ).
Among these dwarfs, four have an HI line detection in our study (MATLAS-785, MATLAS-1750, MATLAS-1824 and MATLAS-1830).

gmw_00008595.name: MATLAS-602
gmw_00008595.overview: MATLAS-602 is a dwarf galaxy that exhibits internal structures, including shell-like features [arXiv:2111.14491]. These shells are asymmetric, which implies a past dwarf-dwarf merger event between two galaxies of unequal masses [arXiv:2111.14491].

sources.summary: The paper points to MATLAS-602 as a dwarf with shell features; its asymmetric shells imply a dwarf-dwarf merger with unequal masses.

quotes.quote: For example, 20 dwarfs identified as dIs have 0.5 < M_ HI /L_B < 1 . These dwarfs all show an elliptical shape as well as internal structures (e.g. MATLAS-1750, MATLAS-519), large star-forming regions or signs of dwarf-dwarf interactions (e.g. the shells of MATLAS-602 discussed in Section section:interactions ).

We observe shell-like features in three dwarfs: MATLAS-824, MATLAS-602, MATLAS-621. The first one exhibits symmetric structures, suggesting a merger event between two dwarfs of similar mass Paudel2017 . The second has asymmetric shell features, implying a different mass for the two dwarf mergers Paudel2018 .

gmw_00008596.name: VTC233002-00
gmw_00008596.overview: VTC233002-00 is a type II quasar located at a redshift of z=1.65 [arXiv:1712.00251]. Following an order-of-magnitude increase in its radio flux density, the active galactic nucleus transitioned from a radio-quiet to a radio-loud state [arXiv:1712.00251].

sources.summary: The paper describes VTC233002-00 as a type II quasar whose radio flux increased by an order of magnitude, changing it from radio-quiet to radio-loud.

quotes.quote: One such AGN, the type II quasar VTC233002-00 at redshift z=1.65 has been recently reported by Mooley et al. (2016). VTC233002-00 was detected at 5.5 mJy at 3 GHz in the CNSS, while the 3 upper limit at 1.4 GHz is 0.4 mJy (mean epoch 1999.2). The order-of-magnitude increase in flux density could be indicative of an enhanced accretion phenomenon leading to the production of a new jet.

A comparison between the radio and optical flux densities indicates that this is a radio loud quasar. However, the value of the radio-loudness parameter ( logR ) calculated before and after the outburst changed from logR <1 to logR = 2.1 , respectively. This indicates that VTC233002-00 has changed its status (look) from radio-quiet to radio-loud source.

gmw_00008597.name: SDP.130
gmw_00008597.overview: SDP.130 is a high-redshift, gravitationally lensed dusty star-forming galaxy selected from the Herschel surveys [arXiv:2207.06967]. It is characterized by a low HCN/CO ratio and a low HCN/FIR upper limit compared to other similar galaxies [arXiv:2207.06967].

sources.summary: The paper includes SDP.130 as a Herschel-selected lensed DSFG with JVLA-observable HCN and reports HCN/FIR and HCN/CO behavior among the lowest-ratio DSFGs in the sample.

quotes.quote: The three sources selected from the Herschel surveys H-ATLAS survey Negrello2010, Negrello2017 and HerMES Oliver2012, Wardlow2012 are SDP.81, SDP.130, and HXMM.02 (also known as Orochi, Ikarashi2011 ). All three targets have HCN(1--0) line observable in the JVLA K-band, robust lens models based on high-resolution ALMA imaging, and existing CO(1--0) measurements.
However, the HCN/FIR upper limits in SDP.130, J1609, and the stacked spectrum are almost 3 lower, indicating that HCN/FIR decreases with increasing L_ FIR .

Our three DSFGs with the lowest HCN/CO ratios (SDP.130, J1202, and J1609), as well as our stack, have FIR/CO ratios a factor of 2--7 lower than either J16359 or SDP.9, while having comparable FIR/HCN ratios (Fig. fig:FIR_HCN ).

gmw_00008598.name: J094531-242831
gmw_00008598.overview: J094531-242831 is a radio-quiet type-II quasar located at a redshift of z=1.65 [arXiv:astro-ph/0603232]. Its host galaxy is extremely blue, indicating ongoing star formation and a lack of old, massive stars [arXiv:astro-ph/0603232].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J094531-242831 as a radio-quiet type-II quasar whose narrow lines resemble radio galaxies; its faint, blue host implies ongoing star formation and few old stars.

quotes.quote: J094531-242831 (hereafter J0945-242) exhibits these bright narrow-emission lines, in the CIV doublet, HeII and CIII ] , all characteristic of a type-II AGN. The radio map shows that there is no radio emission down to a radio flux limit of 0.15 mJy at 5GHz. At a redshift of z=1.65 this is significantly below the typical luminosity of a radio galaxy, thus we confirm that this is a genuine radio-quiet type-II quasar. The line luminosity ratios of the CIV, HeII and CIII ] lines are also consistent with the ratios for radio galaxies, and not the generally lower-luminosity Seyfert-I galaxies and the unobscured quasars (McCarthy 1993).

Thus the host galaxy of J0945-242 appears to be 2.5 mag fainter than that for a typical radio-loud type-II AGN. If this faintness of the host galaxy is caused by extinction from dust then we would expect the blue end of the galaxy spectrum to be fainter, as dust attenuates the blue light more readily than at red wavelengths. However, the host galaxy of J0945-242 is extremely blue, indicative of ongoing star-formation. Therefore, the faintness in the K- band light indicates that the host galaxy has a dearth of old, massive stars, which in turn implies that the galaxy is not yet fully formed at z=1.65 .

gmw_00008599.name: J033246
gmw_00008599.overview: J033246 is a red, high-redshift galaxy with a spectroscopic redshift of z = 1.382 [arXiv:0910.3642, arXiv:1009.5709]. The system appears to be an interacting galaxy [arXiv:0910.3642] and exhibits a submillimeter peak offset from its K-band position [arXiv:1009.5709].

sources.summary: BLAST 1293 is identified with a red high-redshift apparently interacting galaxy; spectroscopy gives z=1.382 despite a higher GOODS-MUSIC redshift estimate.

quotes.quote: BLAST 1293: z_ spec = 1.382 This source does not have a catalogued counterpart at 350 m or 500 m , but the 250 m flux-density is too faint for this to offer a useful redshift constraint. There are two alternative, formally-significant, radio counterparts, and we select VLA 211 in preference to VLA 148. While the 250 m source could be a blend of both objects, our statistically chosen identification transpires to be a bright source at 24 m , while the alternative radio counterpart is undetected in the mid-infrared. As can be seen from Fig. 7, our selected identification is a red, high-redshift, apparently interacting galaxy. The GOODS-MUSIC redshift for this object is z = 1.99 , but our own photometric redshift for this source is z_ phot 1.37 (see last panel of Fig. 5).

sources.summary: The paper reports J033246 as previously spectroscopically identified at z = 1.382, notes its submillimeter peak is offset from its K-band position, and treats it as a lower-redshift BLAST-source exception.

quotes.quote: Three of these seven sources (J033246, J033152, and J033243) were already identified in previous spectroscopic surveys using the Very Large Telescope/FOcal Reducer and low dispersion Spectrograph 2 and the Gemini Near-Infrared Spectrograph vanzella08a,kriek08a at z = 1.382, 2.336 and 2.122 respectively.
J033246 is claimed as an SMG in D10, but its 870 peak is > 30 \ away from its K-band position.
Overall, all BLAST sources (except the lower-redshift J033246) have dust temperatures consistent with the high luminosity end of the SMG distribution.

gmw_00008600.name: J033151
gmw_00008600.overview: J033151 is a high-redshift galaxy with a rest-UV redshift of 1.605 [arXiv:1009.5709]. It exhibits absorption features in the K-band at a redshift of 1.599 and is detected in X-rays above the luminosity expected from its star formation rate [arXiv:1009.5709].

sources.summary: The paper says J033151 has K-band absorption matching an independent rest-UV redshift, lacks the usual H-based identification, and is X-ray detected above its star-formation expectation.

quotes.quote: One of the eight \ redshifts (J033129) would nominally be tentative, but it was spectroscopically identified in the rest-UV independently; the ninth secure redshift which is not based on \ (J033151) has absorption features in the K-band at 1.599 which agree with a rest-UV redshift of 1.605 obtained independently (Swinbank, private communication).
All redshift identifications are based on \ detection except J033151.
Two of the 13 observed sources, J033151 and J033152, are X-ray detected above the luminosities which would correspond with their star formation rates (L _X 10 ^ 44 erg s ^ 1 ).

gmw_00008601.name: J033152
gmw_00008601.overview: J033152 is an active galactic nucleus at a redshift of approximately 2.3 [arXiv:1303.6282, arXiv:1009.5709]. It is a variable radio source whose radio emission is offset from its brightest optical counterpart [arXiv:1303.6282], and it is also detected in X-rays [arXiv:1009.5709].

sources.summary: ID 190 is a variable radio source at photometric redshift 2.296 whose HST counterpart is offset from the brightest emission and whose radio/FIR properties support an AGN identification.

quotes.quote: ID 190: This source has a mean 1.4 GHz flux density of 891 12 Jy. The light curve appears to fluctuate between high and low flux density states on a timescale of order 20 days.

The photometric redshift of z=2.296 bon12 implies a mean radio luminosity of log L_R = 32.1 (cgs). The HST image of this galaxy as seen in Figure Fig:cutouts shows that the radio source is offset from the brightest emission in the field. On the basis of its departure from the radio-FIR correlation, naa+06 classify this source as an AGN. The radio and far-infrared spectral indices (Table Tab:Variables ) and mid-infrared colors (Figure Fig:IRACColors ) are consistent with this identification.

sources.summary: The paper confirms J033152's prior z = 2.336 identification and finds it is X-ray detected, radio-excess bright, and the only observed source with obvious luminous AGN signs.

quotes.quote: Three of these seven sources (J033246, J033152, and J033243) were already identified in previous spectroscopic surveys using the Very Large Telescope/FOcal Reducer and low dispersion Spectrograph 2 and the Gemini Near-Infrared Spectrograph vanzella08a,kriek08a at z = 1.382, 2.336 and 2.122 respectively.

Two of the 13 observed sources, J033151 and J033152, are X-ray detected above the luminosities which would correspond with their star formation rates (L _X 10 ^ 44 erg s ^ 1 ). Only one individual source shows obvious signs of containing a luminous AGN, from its radio flux excess and detection in the X-rays: J033152.

sources.summary: S380 is a compact radio object classified as an AGN by q_24, with X-ray luminosity qualifying it as a galaxy and a low redshift.

quotes.quote: S380 This compact radio object was classified as AGN based on its q_ 24 value of 0.21 ( Norris2006a ). Its X-ray properties qualify it as a galaxy because its X-ray luminosity in the 0.5 keV -- 8 keV band is only L_X=4.6 10^ 31 W ( Lehmer2005 , Szokoly2004 ). It has a spectral index of 0.6 ( Kellermann2008 ) and a redshift of 0.02 ( Wolf2004 ), making it the closest object in our sample.

gmw_00008602.name: J033212
gmw_00008602.overview: J033212 is a high-redshift galaxy with an estimated redshift of 1.81 [arXiv:0910.3642]. It is classified as an ultra-obscured, extremely red K-selected galaxy [arXiv:0910.3642] that lacks a radio detection in VLA data [arXiv:0910.3642, arXiv:1009.5709].

sources.summary: BLAST 193 lacks a VLA counterpart but has a 350 m detection; modeling selected one very red ultra-obscured K-selected galaxy near the far-infrared position.

quotes.quote: BLAST 193: z_ est = 1.81 This 250 m source has a solid detection at 350 m , but has no candidate VLA counterpart within our adopted search radius of 15 arcsec (in fact none within 20 arcsec). We therefore fitted galaxy models to the optical-infrared photometry of all objects in the ISAAC K band image within 15 arcsec of the 250 m position. This search produced five potential counterparts. Only one of these lies at z > 1.5 , and we found this object to also be extremely red. This transpires to be one of only 6 ultra-obscured K selected galaxies in GOODS-South studied in detail by Dunlop et al. (2007; object 1865), and lies only 7 arcsec from the far-infrared position.

sources.summary: The paper notes J033212 is the only galaxy lacking a radio detection, has a high O/H limit, and is poorly fit by a beta-free FIR model because its data are sparse.

quotes.quote: All galaxies except J033212 are also radio detected in VLA data at > 30 .
However two sources (J033249 and J033212) have O/H limits > 9.25, which corresponds to very strong / ( 0.5).

Only J033212 and J033237 are poorly fit to a beta-free model (these are the two galaxies with tentative redshift identifications), since they do not have 70 \ data and have unconstraining upper limits in the FIR.

gmw_00008603.name: Sextans dwarf spheroidal galaxy
gmw_00008603.overview: The Sextans dwarf spheroidal galaxy is a luminous Milky Way satellite located at a heliocentric distance of 86 kpc [arXiv:2302.06071]. It is a dark matter dominated system with a stellar distribution that is well-fitted by an elliptical King profile [arXiv:2302.06071].

sources.summary: The paper studies Sextans with HSC data, describing it as a luminous MW dwarf spheroidal at 86 kpc, dark-matter dominated, and deriving an elliptical King profile with measured core and tidal radii.

quotes.quote: Sextans dwarf spheroidal galaxy (hereafter merely Sextans), ranks among the most luminous "classical" dwarf galaxy satellites of the MW, situated at a heliocentric distance of 86 kpc 1990MNRAS.244P..16I,1991AJ....101..892M,1995MNRAS.277.1354I,1995AJ....110.2166M,2003AJ....126.2840L,10.1093/mnras/stx086 . This galaxy is notable for its extensive spatial extent 1995MNRAS.277.1354I,10.1093/mnras/stw949,10.1093/mnras/stx086,2018ApJ...860...66M , which could potentially indicate tidal disruption, although this interpretation is complicated by the lack of clear evidence such as discernible tidal tails or S-shaped contours in Sextans. From studies on the internal kinematics, Sextans is found to be a dark matter dominated system; the total mass is about 10^8M_ , with a very large mass-to-light ratio ( M/L ) in the range of M/L 500 - 900 depending on the aperture radii 2007ApJ...667L..53W,2009AJ....137.3100W,2008Natur.454.1096S,2011MNRAS.411.1013B .

Thanks to the superb image quality and wide area coverage, the HSC data enables a secure selection of member star candidates based on the colour-magnitude cut, yielding about 10,000 member candidates at magnitudes down to i 24 . We use a likelihood analysis of the two-dimensional distribution of stars to estimate the structural parameters of Sextans taking into account the contamination of foreground halo stars in the MW, and find that the member star distribution is well-fitted by an elliptical King profile with ellipticity 0.25 and the core and tidal radii of R_c=(368.4 8.5)  pc and R_t=(2.54 0.046)  kpc, respectively.

gmw_00008604.name: F563-V2
gmw_00008604.overview: F563-V2 is a low surface brightness galaxy that hosts a strong bar [arXiv:astro-ph/0210152]. The galaxy exhibits limited H-alpha emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0604576], and its rotation curve is better fit by constant-core dark matter halo models than by NFW models [arXiv:astro-ph/0210152].

sources.summary: F563-V2 has one pointing with limited emission, an H-alpha rotation curve generally matching previous HI data, and halo fits that cannot clearly distinguish isothermal from NFW models.

quotes.quote: F563-V2: There was one pointing for this galaxy. The \ fibers are shown on the \ image from ssmpix . The fiber velocities were the average of the , \ and \ lines.

There is little \ emission and only roughly half of the fibers have an \ detection. The position angle was fixed to the value used in Rob . The \ rotation curve generally agrees with the \ rotation curve of DMV .

sources.summary: The paper reports that NFW models overpredict F563-V2's inner slope, constant-core models fit better, and its bar may drive non-circular motions.

quotes.quote: However, in a number of cases, the NFW model overpredicts the inner slope of the rotation curve: UGC 731, UGC 4325, UGC 4499, and F563-V2, and in two cases, UGC 5721 and UGC 11707, the NFW model seems to fare better than the pseudo-isothermal model.
Only for four galaxies in our sample do models with =0 yield significantly better fits than for =1 independent of \ (UGC 4325, UGC 4499, F563-V2 and F568-3).

Interestingly, 3 out of the 4 galaxies which are poorly fit by an NFW halo have strong bars (UGC 4499, F563-V2 and F568-3), suggesting that the non-circular motions associated with bars may have contributed to the poor fits.

gmw_00008605.name: F568-3
gmw_00008605.overview: F568-3 is a low surface brightness galaxy that features a strong bar structure [arXiv:astro-ph/0210152]. Its observed H-alpha rotation curve is poorly fit by both pseudo-isothermal and NFW dark matter halo models, which may be due to bar-driven non-circular motions [arXiv:astro-ph/0210152].

sources.summary: F568-3 has one pointing, roughly 60 percent fiber emission, a fixed long-slit position angle, and an H-alpha rotation curve consistent with earlier long-slit and HI curves.

quotes.quote: F568-3: There was one pointing for this galaxy and the fiber positions are shown on an \ image (W.J.G. de Blok 2005, private communication). The fiber velocities were the average of the \ and the \ lines. \ emission was detected in roughly 60 \ of the fibers. The position angle was fixed to the MRdB and Rob long-slit value. The \ rotation curve is consistent with the long-slit \ curve of dBMR and the \ curve of DMV .

sources.summary: The paper describes F568-3 as poorly fit by both pseudo-isothermal and NFW models; constant-core models perform better than NFW, possibly because of bar-driven non-circular motions.

quotes.quote: For F568-3 and UGC 8490 both the pseudo-isothermal and the NFW give poor fits to the observed rotation curves.
Only for four galaxies in our sample do models with =0 yield significantly better fits than for =1 independent of \ (UGC 4325, UGC 4499, F563-V2 and F568-3).

Interestingly, 3 out of the 4 galaxies which are poorly fit by an NFW halo have strong bars (UGC 4499, F563-V2 and F568-3), suggesting that the non-circular motions associated with bars may have contributed to the poor fits.

gmw_00008606.name: NGC 6051
gmw_00008606.overview: NGC 6051 is the central dominant cD galaxy of the poor cluster AWM 4 [arXiv:0809.2036, arXiv:astro-ph/0608491]. The galaxy hosts an active galactic nucleus and radio source [arXiv:0909.0600, arXiv:astro-ph/0608491] whose jets have transported metal-enriched gas out of the galactic core [arXiv:1010.0610].

sources.summary: The paper links NGC 6051's radio jets and AGN outburst to uplift of enriched intracluster gas, finding super-solar abundances along the jets and longer-timescale metal mixing outward from the galaxy.

quotes.quote: While the cluster core is generally enriched to near--solar abundances, we find evidence of super--solar abundances correlated with the radio jets, extending 35 kpc from the core of the central dominant galaxy NGC 6051 along its minor axis. We conclude that the enriched gas has been transported out of the central galaxy through the action of the radio source. We estimate the excess mass of iron in the entrained gas to be 1.4 10^6 , and find that this can be produced in the core of NGC 6051 within the timescale of the AGN outburst. The energy required to transport this gas to its current location is 4.5 10^ 57 erg, a significant fraction of the estimated total mechanical energy output of the AGN, though this estimate is dependent on the degree of enrichment of the uplifted gas. The larger near--solar abundance region is also compatible with enrichment by metals mixed outward from NGC 6051 over a much longer timescale.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 6051 as AWM 4's cD galaxy, uses it as the system velocity center, notes X-ray emission centered on it, and remeasures its underestimated SDSS photometry.

quotes.quote: NGC 6051, V_ T ^0= 12.93 mag RC3 , located at z=0.031755 0.000033 1999MNRAS.305..259W , is the cD of AWM 4. subsequently identified 28 members brighter than R =15.5 mag. Most are early-type galaxies, concentrated in the center and with a smooth Gaussian velocity distribution centered at the velocity of NGC 6051.
This has been confirmed by the latest X-ray observations, with XMM- Newton and at a resolution of 6 arcsec 2005MNRAS.357.1134O , where the X-ray emission is centred on NGC 6051.

By comparing the so-called `model' magnitudes from the SDSS-DR6 with our own measurements, we find that NGC 6051 is reported 0.67 magnitude too faint, while other galaxies down to 15 mag are systematically fainter by 0.1 to 0.2 magnitude.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 6051 as AWM 4's dominant BCG, brighter than surrounding galaxies, hosting a powerful radio source and a compact cool corona.

quotes.quote: 28 galaxy members have been identified, most are absorption line systems with a strong concentration of early-type galaxies in the center and with a smooth gaussian velocity distribution centered at the velocity of NGC 6051; the velocity dispersion of the system is 439^ +93 _ 58 km s ^ 1 Koranyi.ea:02 . NGC 6051 is considerably brighter than the galaxies around it and recent deep R band imaging Zibetti.ea:09 has established the fossil nature of this system. NGC 6051 harbors a powerful radio source with 1.4 GHz flux of 607 22 mJy from the NVSS catalog, Condon.ea:98 .

The \ data could not rule out the presence of low-entropy gas at scales smaller than the inner 20 kpc and indeed a recent \ observation has revealed the presence of a cool ( 1 keV) compact and extended corona associated with the BCG NGC 6051.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 6051 as AWM4's dominant central elliptical/cD galaxy, expected to enrich the ICM, and notes its powerful AGN/radio source interacting with cluster gas.

quotes.quote: This poor relaxed cluster consists of 28 galaxies centered at the dominant elliptical NGC 6051 (Koranyi Geller 2002).
The central galaxy hosts a powerful AGN (4C +24.36) which could be driving the gas motions in this cluster.
There is a powerful radio source in NGC 6051 which clearly interacts with the ICM (e.g. O'Sullivan et al. 2005).

gmw_00008607.name: NGC 1169
gmw_00008607.overview: NGC 1169 is a barred spiral galaxy that exhibits active star formation [arXiv:1204.6212]. Within its bar region, the galaxy displays a positive gas abundance gradient alongside a notably high dispersion in its abundance values [arXiv:1204.6212].

sources.summary: The paper finds limited Balmer coverage for NGC 1169, a positive bar-region gas abundance gradient, and unusually high dispersion in its abundance values.

quotes.quote: For NGC 1169, we observe only \ except at 5 galactocentric radii.

If we consider all the detected emission in the bar region at a 3- significance, for the star formation galaxies, we detect a positive gradient for three galaxies (NGC 1169, NGC 2523 and NGC 2935) and a negative gradient is observed for two galaxies (NGC 1832 and NGC 2962).
We find gradients in the range [ 3.7,1.4 ] (normalized to R _ 25 ) with the exception of NGC 1169, which has a very high dispersion in the abundance values.

gmw_00008608.name: SN 2015J host galaxy
gmw_00008608.overview: The SN 2015J host galaxy is a compact dwarf galaxy located at a distance of approximately 24.2 Mpc [arXiv:1711.08655]. It has an extended structure with a physical size of about 1 kpc and exhibits a diffuse surrounding brightness [arXiv:1711.08655].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the formerly orphan SN 2015J host as an extended compact dwarf galaxy about 1 kpc across at 24.2 Mpc, based on VLT and Magellan imaging.

quotes.quote: At first, it appeared to be an orphan SN candidate, i.e. without any clear identification of its host galaxy. Here, we present the analysis of the observations carried out by the VLT 8-m class telescope with the FORS2 camera in the R band and the Magellan telescope (6.5 m) equipped with the IMACS Short-Camera (V and I filters) and the FourStar camera (Ks filter) . We show that SN 2015J resides in what appears to be a very compact galaxy establishing a relation between the SN event and its natural host.

After subtracting the PSF from the image, we were left with an image of residuals (Figure figuresubtractions ) in which all the star-like objects are removed. Note that large deviations (from what expected for a point-like object) appear at the location of the source and this is convincing evidence we are dealing with an extended object Note that a diffuse brightness surrounds the target and, based on the K image of Figure figure2 , an emission peak (the knot with magnitude 18.77 0.18 ) is also present in a position located at the South-East from the core. . As a matter of fact the SExtractor code classified the target as a galaxy (with ellipticity parameter 0.127 and S/G classifier output of 0.03 ). The extraction radius used in order to encompass the visible halo of the galaxy is 4 , corresponding to a linear length of 0.5 kpc for an estimated distance of 24.2 Mpc as that of SN 2015J.
Optical and near-IR observations allowed us to discover of the SN host galaxy which appears to be a compact dwarf galaxy with a size of 1 kpc at a distance of 24.2 Mpc.

gmw_00008609.name: KKH 18
gmw_00008609.overview: KKH 18 is a nearby isolated dwarf spheroidal galaxy candidate [arXiv:2106.02672]. The galaxy possesses a detected H I halo and displays bright features in its image, with no obvious signs of dust [arXiv:2106.02672].

sources.summary: The paper describes KKH 18 as a nearby isolated dwarf spheroidal candidate near M81, with an H I halo and bright features in its image.

quotes.quote: I think the nearest large galaxy to KKH 18 is M 81, 1.3 Mpc away . The images of these three objects look to me like dwarf spheroidals, with no obvious signs of dust revealed by obscuration or scattered starlight. All three have detected HI halos, suggesting isolated evolution appropriate for their isolated present positions , though I don't know whether the HI is contributing to star formation. The image of KKH 18 does show bright blobs of something.

gmw_00008610.name: host galaxy of PS1-14bj
gmw_00008610.overview: The host galaxy of PS1-14bj is a faint galaxy detected in deep stacked imaging [arXiv:1605.05235]. Spectral modeling indicates a low stellar mass of approximately 10 to the power of 8.5 solar masses, while narrow emission lines reveal that the galaxy is actively star-forming [arXiv:1605.05235].

sources.summary: The paper detects a faint PS1-14bj host in deep stacks; SED fitting gives low stellar mass, late spectra show star formation, and its luminosity and mass are typical of H-poor SLSN hosts.

quotes.quote: To search for a host galaxy, we stack the PS1/MDS data from the four observing seasons prior to the detection of PS1-14bj. No host is detected in the ``standard'' deep stacks (consisting of the 80 observations with best image quality), but by stacking all the available data (about 200 individual observations/filter) we are able to go significantly deeper at the expense of worse FWHM in the resulting stacks. A faint galaxy is detected at the position of PS1-14bj in all filters; as an example, the \ stack is shown in Figure fig:hostgal .

Fitting a galaxy model to the SED using the FAST code kvl+09 , we find a best-fit galaxy mass of ( M_ gal /M_ ) = 8.5 0.5 . Other galaxy properties going into the model grid (such as extinction, stellar population age and star formation rate) are not well constrained by our relatively noisy galaxy photometry. The detection of narrow [ O 3 ] 5007 and H emission from the host galaxy in our late time spectra indicates that the host galaxy is star forming. While we do not detect narrow H , we can calculate a lower limit on the star formation rate from the H flux assuming no host galaxy extinction. Using H and the relation from ken98 we calculate a star formation rate of 0.2 M_ yr ^ 1 .

gmw_00008611.name: J1435 + 0008
gmw_00008611.overview: J1435 + 0008 is a double quasar system that is considered a strong gravitationally lensed candidate [arXiv:astro-ph/0210644]. The two images share a small redshift difference, common line ratios, and consistent colors [arXiv:astro-ph/0210644].

sources.summary: The paper presents J1435 + 0008 as a strong lensed-QSO candidate, with matching spectra, small redshift difference, common line ratios, and SDSS colors consistent between the two images.

quotes.quote: This is a good lensed QSO candidate, with a small redshift difference and an unusual ratio of Ly- and NV which is common to both spectra. This QSO pair also appears in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) Early Data Release stoughton02 . Table EDR gives the SDSS photometry for this pair, together with the differences in apparent magnitudes and the associated error. It can be seen that the colours of the two QSOs are consistent with being the same to substantially higher accuracy than was possible with the 2QZ photographic photometry, although there does seem to be some some evidence for a systematic difference in colour with waveband. This may be taken as evidence against the system being lensed, or it may be accounted for either by spectral variability or by differential extinction between the light paths.

gmw_00008612.name: J0150 2921
gmw_00008612.overview: J0150 2921 is a candidate single gravitationally lensed quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0210644]. The system features several bright nearby galaxies, three of which have measured redshifts [arXiv:astro-ph/0210644].

sources.summary: The paper treats J0150 2921 as one of the strongest single-lensed-QSO candidates despite a broad absorption difference, and notes nearby galaxies with measured redshifts.

quotes.quote: This is possibly one of the most enigmatic pairs of spectra obtained. The spectra have good signal-to-noise ratio and appear identical in every respect apart from one gross feature: the presence of a broad absorption line system in one of the spectra. For reasons described in section timedelay we do not believe that this should be taken as evidence against the system being a lensed QSO, and because of the otherwise striking similarity between the spectra in these observations with good signal-to-noise we regard this as being one of the systems most likely to be a single lensed QSO. This system also has a number of bright galaxies in the vicinity, of which three have had redshifts measured by the 2dF Galaxy Redshift Survey colless03 . The positions and redshifts of the two QSOs and of the three galaxies are shown in Fig. fchart .

gmw_00008613.name: J0135 3021
gmw_00008613.overview: J0135 3021 is a pair of objects classified as a possible gravitationally lensed quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0210644]. Small differences in the ultraviolet spectra of the pair may be due to atmospheric refraction and slit alignment errors during observations [arXiv:astro-ph/0210644].

sources.summary: The paper keeps J0135 3021 as a possible lensed QSO, attributing small ultraviolet spectral differences partly to observing geometry and calling for more observations.

quotes.quote: There are some small differences visible in the ultraviolet parts of the spectra of this pair which lead to the somewhat high value of redshift difference given in Table ratios . However, these spectra were taken simultaneously, with the slit rotated to the position angle defined by the coordinates of the two QSOs, and the observations presented here were made with the pair within about thirty degrees of the zenith. Slight errors in aligning the slit with the pair could result in a small differential loss of light owing to atmospheric refraction which may contribute to the small differences at < 4500 . At this stage we still regard this pair as possibly being a lensed QSO, although further observations are needed.

gmw_00008614.name: J0223 2949
gmw_00008614.overview: J0223 2949 is a possible gravitationally lensed system consisting of a pair with nearly identical spectra [arXiv:astro-ph/0210644]. The system has a redshift difference consistent with zero and features several bright galaxies in its immediate vicinity [arXiv:astro-ph/0210644].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0223 2949 as a possible lensed system with nearly identical spectra, zero-consistent redshift difference, and nearby bright galaxies with measured redshifts.

quotes.quote: This pair has spectra identical within the noise except in the CIV line, where there is a residual difference whose maximum is about one-third the maximum of the emission-line. The other emission lines and the detailed continuum shape are extremely similar, however, and the redshift difference is consistent with zero at the 2-sigma level. We regard this as being a possible lensed system that requires further observations. This system also has a number of bright galaxies in the vicinity (Fig.  fchart ), of which ten have had redshifts measured by the 2dF Galaxy Redshift Survey colless03 .

gmw_00008615.name: J0017+5312
gmw_00008615.overview: J0017+5312 is a core-jet quasar located at a redshift of z=2.574 [arXiv:1111.3710]. Its radio spectrum turns over around 5 GHz, and it displays a weak, steep-spectrum northwest extension alongside a core that showed shrinking in size between 1994 and 2005 [arXiv:1111.3710].

sources.summary: The paper treats J0017+5312 as a doubtful CSO and likely core-jet quasar, with compact VLBI components, a relic-like extension, and apparent contraction from jet-knot motion.

quotes.quote: The core-jet sourceJ0017+5312 is associated with a quasar at redshift z=2.574 (1 mas = 7.926 pc) . Its total flux density spectrum turns over at around 5 GHz . The 1.5-GHz NVSS image shows a compact component with a flux density of 380 mJy and a weak extension 30 ( 240 kpc) northwest of the core (Figure fig:j0017 b) with a peak intensity of about 8 times the rms noise. However, this extended component is not detected in the VLA images at 8 and 43 GHz (VLA/NRAO archive) suggesting a steep spectrum.

Although the possibility of a CSO classification for J0017+5312 is not fully excluded, we tentatively identify J0017+5312 as a core-jet source. Besides the observational data in 2005, also the archival 8-GHz VLBA data of J0017+5312 observed in 1994 has been mapped. A direct comparison of the radio images in epochs 1994 and 2005 shows a shrinking of the source size (Figure fig:j0017 a).

gmw_00008616.name: J0132+5620
gmw_00008616.overview: J0132+5620 is a compact symmetric object radio galaxy with a symmetric double-source structure aligned in the East-West direction [arXiv:1111.3710]. It features a flat-spectrum central component that is likely associated with the galactic core, situated between two radio lobes [arXiv:1111.3710].

sources.summary: The paper confirms J0132+5620 as a CSO, identifying a likely central core between two lobes and using multi-epoch VLBI to study its motions.

quotes.quote: The optical redshift of the host galaxy of J0132+5620 is not available in the literature. This source was included in the COINS sample and has been observed with 1 mas resolution during 1997 and 2002 . It displays a symmetric double-source structure in the East-West direction at 2.3 GHz (VCS archive). Figure fig:j0132 shows the 8.4-GHz images of J0132+5620 derived from the new 2009 observations and the previous ones made in 2000, 2002 and 2005 (Peck Taylor 2000; Gugliucci et al. 2005; the current paper).

Since B2 is the only flat-spectrum VLBI component and lies near the geometric center of J0132+5620, it is most likely that B2 is associated with the core. Future simultaneous VLBI observations at 5 and 8.4 GHz with high sensitivity are needed to confirm the core identification of B2 .

gmw_00008617.name: J0518+4730
gmw_00008617.overview: J0518+4730 is a compact symmetric object with a triple morphology and no known optical redshift [arXiv:1111.3710]. Its central component is highly compact with a steep spectrum, likely representing the innermost jet emission near the core [arXiv:1111.3710].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J0518+4730 as a triple-morphology CSO, describing steep-spectrum components and interpreting the central feature as innermost jet emission near the core.

quotes.quote: CSO source J0518+4730 is another source from the COINS sample but it has no known optical redshift. The VLBI image derived from the present 8.4-GHz data displays a triple structure (Figure fig:j0518 ), in good agreement with previous observations at similar frequencies (Peck Taylor 2000). The eastern and western components show a slight misalignment with a difference of 17 in position angle. Gugliucci et al. (2005) classified J0518+4730 as a core-jet source and identified the easternmost component A as the core.

The central component B is the most compact component, but it also has a steep spectrum with ^ 15.4 _ 5.0 =0.7 (Paper II). B is most likely associated with the innermost jet in the vicinity of the core. Therefore, J0518+4730 may be classified as a CSO with a triple morphology.

gmw_00008618.name: J1335+5844
gmw_00008618.overview: J1335+5844 is an active galaxy with a faint host galaxy that has an estimated photometric redshift of 0.57 [arXiv:1111.3710]. It is classified as a compact symmetric object and a high-frequency peaker, featuring a triple VLBI structure with a flat-spectrum central component [arXiv:1111.3710].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1335+5844 as a CSO/HFP source, with a faint host, triple VLBI structure, a flat-spectrum central component, and compact hot-spot properties.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy of J1335+5844 (also named as 4C + 58.26, m_V=22 ) was found to be too faint to measure the redshift. Stanghellini et al. (2009) estimated a photometric redshift of 0.57 for this source (1 mas = 6.331 pc). The source has been identified as a High Frequency Peaker (HFP) with a turnover frequency at about 5 GHz . The 8.4-GHz image derived from the VLBA observation in 2004 (see also Fig. 1 in Dallacasa et al. 2005, and Fig. 2 in Stanghellini et al. 2009) is shown in the left panel of Figure fig:j1335 .

The recent multi-frequency VLBA observations of J1335+5844 made in 2010 confirm the flat spectral index of C (D. Dallacasa, private communication). Therefore, we consider J1335+5844 as a CSO in light of the compact symmetric structures with respect to a central component.

gmw_00008619.name: J1734+0926
gmw_00008619.overview: J1734+0926 is a compact symmetric object galaxy located at a redshift of 0.735 [arXiv:1111.3710]. Its radio structure features two edge-brightened hot spots separated by a projected distance of approximately 100 pc, with an undetected weak core at its geometric center [arXiv:1111.3710].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1734+0926 as a CSO galaxy with two edge-brightened hot spots, an undetected weak core, and an inferred kinematic age near 1300 years.

quotes.quote: CSO source J1734+0926 (PKS 1732+094) is associated with a galaxy of magnitude m_ R_C =20.8 and at redshift of 0.735 (1 mas = 7.086 pc) . The source is identified as a CSO based on the morphology with two edge-brightened hot spots ( ^ 15.4 _ 2.3 (A)= 1.1 , ^ 15.4 _ 2.3 (B)= 1.3 : Paper II) and an extension toward the geometric center, where the core is too weak to be detected Sta99,Pec00 . Figure fig:j1734 (top) displays the radio structure of J1734+0926. The projected separation between two hot spots is about 100 pc. The positional variation of the hot spot A1 relative to B1 is displayed in the inset of Figure fig:j1734 (bottom).

gmw_00008620.name: J1756+5748
gmw_00008620.overview: J1756+5748 is a high-redshift core-jet quasar with an optical magnitude of 18 [arXiv:1111.3710]. Its radio structure is characterized by a flat-spectrum western core and a series of bright knots forming an eastward-moving jet [arXiv:1111.3710].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J1756+5748 as a high-redshift core-jet quasar with a knotty east-west radio jet, flat-spectrum core, superluminal motions, and downstream kinematic ages.

quotes.quote: Core-jet source J1756+5748 has been identified with a high-redshift quasar ( z=2.11 ; Henstock et al. 1997; 1 mas = 8.201 pc) of magnitude 18. The top panel in Figure fig:j1756 shows the 8.4-GHz image of J1756+5748 obtained from the observations in 2009. The radio structure is characterized by a series of bright knots in the East-West direction, consistent with previous observations at 5 GHz Tay94,Bri08 . From the observations reported in Paper II, the knot D1 at the western extreme of the source has the flattest spectral index ^ 15.4GHz _ 8.4GHz =0.0 0.1 , and is identified as the radio core.
J1756+5748 is classified as a core-jet source. Figure fig:j1756 (bottom) displays the 2-dimensional distribution of the jet components and their general eastern motion relative to D1 .

gmw_00008621.name: J2203+1007
gmw_00008621.overview: J2203+1007 is a compact symmetric object and high-frequency peaker radio source likely associated with a galaxy at a redshift of 1.005 [arXiv:1111.3710]. Its radio morphology displays double steep-spectrum components with sharp outer edges and an undetected central core [arXiv:1111.3710].

sources.summary: The paper describes J2203+1007 as a likely galaxy-associated CSO/HFP source with double steep-spectrum components, sharp-edged lobes, and a weak undetected central core.

quotes.quote: The CSO source J2203+1007 is likely associated with a galaxy of magnitude 22.02 at a redshift of 1.005 (1 mas = 7.830 pc) . The radio spectrum of the source shows a convex spectrum with a peak at 5 GHz, which identifies it as a HFP . The VLBI images of the source display a double-component morphology at 1.7 and 2.3 GHz. At 5.0 GHz and higher frequencies, both the eastern and western component shows a steep spectrum with ^ 15.4 _ 5.0 =1.1 0.2 (East) and ^ 15.4 _ 5.0 =1.3 0.2 (West).

The morphology of both components (Figure fig:j2203 ) display sharp outer edges and an extension toward the geometric center similar with that of J1734+0926. All the lobes and internal jet components are found to have steep spectral indices between 5.0 and 15.4 GHz ranging from 1.2 to 1.7 (Paper II). The central core probably lies between components B and D2 but it is too weak to be detected.

gmw_00008622.name: J2312+3847
