quotes.quote: The three exceptions are the DLAs towards TXS0311+430, TXS1313+200 and the z_ abs = 2.752 absorber towards TXS2039+187.

If this is indeed the case, then the DLA towards TXS1313+200 and the z_ abs = 2.752 absorber towards TXS2039+187 whose metallicities appear to be 1/3 -- 1/10 and 1/3 of the solar value respectively, may also have low spin temperatures. It will therefore be interesting to confirm the unusually high metallicities of these DLAs with higher resolution spectroscopy. If they indeed arise in massive galaxies, as may be expected from the mass-metallicity relation observed in high redshift galaxies (Savaglio et al. 2005; Erb et al. 2006; Liu et al. 2008), Hubble Space Telescope images may be able to confirm their morphologies and luminosities.

gmw_00008670.name: PKS B0625 545
gmw_00008670.overview: PKS B0625 545 is an elliptical, Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy located at the center of the A3395 cluster [arXiv:2012.08775, arXiv:1109.5688]. It is classified as a wide-angle tailed source with bent radio jets that interact with the surrounding intracluster medium [arXiv:2012.08775, arXiv:1109.5688].

sources.summary: EMU ES J0626--5432 is the central A3395 FR I radio galaxy PKS 0625--545, with a WISEA host, bright radio fluxes, a southward jet expansion, and X-ray structures tracing ICM interaction.

quotes.quote: The bright radio galaxy EMU ES J0626--5432 (PKS 0625--545) is a Fanaroff-Riley class I (FR I) 1974MNRAS.167P..31F source located in the centre of the A3395 cluster and extends over 5.5 arcmin (see Fig. fig:mainsource ). Its host galaxy is WISEA J062649.57--543234.4 ( z = 0.052). Radio fluxes from 145 MHz to 8.5 GHz as well as 148 GHz are listed in NED. The SUMSS 843 MHz flux is catalogued as 5 Jy and the Australia Telescope Compact Array (ATCA) 1.4 GHz flux as 3.1 Jy 2011ApJ...743...78L . To the south, just as it starts to exit the denser X-ray gas (see Fig. fig:chandra_s1 ), the tip of the radio jet starts to expand spherically and the brightness of the emission drops.

There is some indication of a depression in the X-ray surface brightness in the region of the round, southern tip, but it is of low significance 2011ApJ...743...78L . The northern tip appears to end where there is an excess of emission in the smooth model residual map. The edge-filtered map confirms that the two ends of the radio source are located where there are steep gradients in the X-ray surface brightness.

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS B0625 545 as an elliptical WAT radio galaxy in A3395, with bent jets, no detected X-ray cavities, and a well-measured radio spectrum.

quotes.quote: The ATCA images at 1348 and 2374 MHz show that the most prominent source in the field is the wide-angle tailed (WAT) source PKS B0625 545 (MRC B0625 545) which is identified with an elliptical galaxy at a redshift of 0.05174 (Teague, Carter, Gray 1990) (Figure fig:A3395_WAT_SuperCOSMOS_overlaid_ATCA_radio_contours ). Both the oppositely-directed jets bend initially towards the west and then again bend sharply further down the flow at 90 kpc from the host galaxy so that the emission is approximately along the north-south axis. Douglass et al. (2008) have observed similar bends in the WAT source in Abell 562 and have interpreted the bends further down the flow as the regions where the effects of buoyancy become important.

It may be worth noting that there is no evidence of X-ray cavities associated with the regions of radio emission. The flux densities of the WAT galaxy at a number of frequencies from low-resolution observations are listed in Table tab:radio_flux , including the values listed by Burgess Hunstead (2006), and estimates from the ATCA images presented in this paper. The flux density estimated from the ATCA image at 1348 MHz is very close to the measurement at 1410 MHz, suggesting that the interferometric observations at this frequency have not missed any significant amount of flux density.

gmw_00008671.name: HT galaxy
gmw_00008671.overview: The HT galaxy is a head-tail radio source associated with a galaxy at a redshift of 0.05955 [arXiv:1109.5688]. It has a linear size of 90 kpc, with measured peak and total flux densities at 1348 MHz, 2374 MHz, and 843 MHz [arXiv:1109.5688].

sources.summary: The paper describes the HT galaxy on A3395 W's periphery as a 90 kpc head-tail radio source with measured 1348 and 2374 MHz flux densities.

quotes.quote: Besides the WAT galaxy, there is a head-tail (HT) source located at RA(J2000): 06^ h 25^ m 56.7^ s and Dec.(J2000): 54^ d 27^ 50^ which is associated with a galaxy at a redshift of 0.05955 (Teague, Carter, Gray 1990) and is shown in Figure fig:A3395_SuperCOSMOS_optical_image . The extent of the HT galaxy is smaller than the WAT, with an angular size of 80^ including the diffuse emission, which corresponds to a linear size of 90 kpc at the redshift of the galaxy. The peak and total flux densities of the HT source at 1348 MHz are 25 mJy beam ^ 1 and 150 mJy. The tail is not as well imaged at 2374 MHz where the peak and total flux densities are 8 mJy beam ^ 1 and 70 mJy respectively. MOST observations show that the peak and total flux densities of the HT source are 135 mJy beam ^ 1 and 299 mJy respectively at 843 MHz (Reid 2000).

gmw_00008672.name: Pegasus III
gmw_00008672.overview: Pegasus III is an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy satellite located in the outer halo of the Milky Way [arXiv:1608.04934]. It is composed of an old, metal-poor stellar population situated at a heliocentric distance of approximately 215 kpc [arXiv:1608.04934].

sources.summary: The paper measures Peg III as an old, metal-poor ultra-faint dwarf satellite at 215 kpc, with revised structure, low metallicity, velocity dispersion, and high mass-to-light ratio.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Properties of Peg III Parameter & Value & Unit _ J2000 & 22 24 24.48 & h m s _ J2000 & +05 24 18.0 & ^

quotes.quote: Pegasus III (Peg III) is one of the few known ultra-faint stellar systems in the outer halo of the Milky Way. We present the results from a follow-up campaign with Magellan/IMACS and Keck/DEIMOS. Deep stellar photometry down to r_0 25 mag at 50 completeness level has allowed accurate measurements of its photometric and structural properties. The color-magnitude diagram of Peg III confirms that the stellar system is well described by an old ( 12 Gyr) and metal-poor ( [ Fe/H ] 2.0 dex) stellar population at a heliocentric distance of 215 12 kpc. The revised half-light radius r_ h =53 14 pc, ellipticity =0.38^ +0.22 _ 0.38 , and total luminosity M_ V =-3.4 0.4 are in good agreement with the values quoted in our previous paper.

The Ca II triplet lines of the brightest member stars indicate that Peg III contains stars with metallicity as low as [ Fe/H ] = 2.55 0.15 dex. Peg III has a systemic velocity of 222.9 2.6 km s ^ 1 and a velocity dispersion of 5.4^ +3.0 _ 2.5 km s ^ 1 . The inferred dynamical mass within the half-light radius is 1.4^ +3.0 _ 1.1 10^6 M_ and the mass-to-light ratio M/L _ V = 1470^ +5660 _ 1240 M_ /L_ , providing further evidence that Peg III is a dwarf galaxy satellite.

gmw_00008673.name: Psc II
gmw_00008673.overview: Pisces II is an ultra-faint dwarf spheroidal galaxy located approximately 175 to 183 kiloparsecs away [arXiv:2105.11488]. It contains a dominant, metal-poor stellar population older than 12 billion years [arXiv:2105.11488], including a carbon-enhanced metal-poor star [arXiv:1807.01542] and at least one RR Lyrae variable [arXiv:2105.11488].

sources.summary: The paper studies Pisces II variable stars, deriving a 175 kpc distance, old metal-poor stellar population, metallicity spread, and no isodensity evidence for connection to Pegasus III.

quotes.quote: Using the intensity-averaged apparent magnitude of the bona-fide RRab star in each galaxy we estimate distance moduli of (m-M)_ 0 = 21.22 0.14 mag (d= 175 11 kpc) and 21.21 0.23 mag (d=174 18 kpc) for Pisces II and Pegasus III, respectively. Tests performed to disentangle the actual nature of variables with an uncertain classification led us to conclude that they most likely are bright, long period and very metal poor RRab members of their respective hosts. This may indicate that Pisces II and Pegasus III contain a dominant old stellar population (t > 12 Gyr) with metallicity < [ Fe/H ] >- 1.8 dex along with, possibly, a minor, more metal-poor component, as supported by the V , B-V color-magnitude diagrams of the two UFDs and their spectroscopically confirmed members. The metallicity spread that we derived from our data sample is 0.4 dex in both systems.

The Psc II UFD [ R.A.(J2000) = 22h58m31s, DEC.(J2000) = 5 ^ 57 ^ 9 ^ ; l = 79.21 ^ , b = 47.11 ^ ] was discovered in the southern Galactic portion of the SDSS Segue survey ( yan09 ) by bel10 , and later studied in more detail by san12 and kir15 . Using deep wide-field photometry obtained with Megacam at the Magellan Clay telescope, san12 estimated the structural and photometric parameters of Psc II. From the mean magnitude of the HB they derived the distance modulus of 21.31 0.17 mag, corresponding to a heliocentric distance of 183 15 kpc. san12 also estimated a half-light radius of 1.1 0.2 arcmin, which delimits the region containing the bulk of the Psc II stars and, at the estimated heliocentric distance, corresponds to a linear extension of 58 10 pc and an absolute magnitude M _V = 4.1 0.4 mag for Psc II.

sources.summary: The paper confirms Pisces II as a dwarf galaxy from Keck/DEIMOS spectroscopy, reporting velocity and marginal metallicity dispersions, consistency with the luminosity-metallicity relation, and a moderate mass-to-light ratio.

quotes.quote: We present Keck/DEIMOS spectroscopy of stars in the recently discovered Milky Way satellites Hydra II, Pisces II, and Laevens 1 . We measured a velocity dispersion of _ ^ + km s ^ 1 for Pisces II, but we did not resolve the velocity dispersions of Hydra II or Laevens 1 . We marginally resolved the metallicity dispersions of Hydra II and Pisces II but not Laevens 1 . Furthermore, Hydra II and Pisces II obey the luminosity--metallicity relation for Milky Way dwarf galaxies ( Fe/H = and , respectively), whereas Laevens 1 does not ( Fe/H = ). The kinematic and chemical properties suggest that Hydra II and Pisces II are dwarf galaxies, and Laevens 1 is a globular cluster.

The mass-to-light ratio for Pisces II is _ ^ + M_ /L_ . It is not among the most dark matter-dominated dwarf galaxies, but it is still worthy of inclusion in the search for gamma rays from dark matter self-annihilation.

sources.summary: The paper describes Pisces II as an old ultra-faint dwarf likely linked to first-star relics, with one confirmed CEMP-no star among its few observed stars.

quotes.quote: A probable carbon enhanced metal-poor (CEMP) star, , was discovered recently in the ultra-faint (UFD) galaxy Pisces II. This galaxy is supposed to be very old, suspected to include dark matter, and likely formed the bulk of its stars before the reionisation of the Universe. New abundances have been obtained from observations of at the Kueyen ESO VLT telescope, using the high-efficiency spectrograph: X-Shooter. We found that is a CEMP-no star with [ Fe/H ] =--2.60 dex. Careful measurements of the CH and bands confirm the enhancement of the C abundance ( [ C/Fe ] =+1.23). This cool giant has very probably undergone extra mixing and thus its original C abundance could be even higher. Nitrogen, O, Na, and Mg are also strongly enhanced, but from Ca to Ni the ratios [ X/Fe ] are similar to those observed in classical very metal-poor stars. With its low Ba abundance ( [ Ba/Fe ] =--1.10 dex) is a CEMP-no star. No variation in the radial velocity could be detected between 2015 and 2017. The pattern of the elements has a shape similar to the pattern found in galactic CEMP-no stars like CS 22949-037 ( [ Fe/H ] =--4.0) or SDSS J1349+1407 ( [ Fe/H ] =--3.6). The existence of a CEMP-no star in the UFD galaxy Pisc II suggests that this small galaxy likely hosted zero-metallicity stars.

The existence of a CEMP-no star in the UFD galaxy Pisces II (with L 10^ 4 L_ ) is consistent with theoretical predictions of cosmological models e.g. SalvadoriST15 and hydro-dynamical simulations e.g. JeonBB17 suggesting that these small galaxies might be the living relics of star-forming mini-halos hosting the first stars. In these studies, long-lived CEMP-no stars form in gaseous environments predominantly imprinted by the chemical products of primordial low-energy faint supernovae (SNe) e.g. BonifacioLC03,NomotoUM03 . The observed [ C/Fe ] = +1.23 and [ Fe/H ] = --2.6 of Pisces II 10694 suggest that its birth environment was likely polluted by both primordial faint SNe and normal Pop II stars exploding as core-collapse supernovae ( SalvadoriST15 section 4, and also deBennassutiSS17 ). investigated the frequency of CEMP-no stars in dwarf galaxies with different luminosities. One of their key results is that the probability of observing CEMP-no stars, imprinted by primordial faint SNe, increases with decreasing galaxy luminosity and, on average, this probability is an order of magnitude higher in UFDs than in more massive classical Sculptor-like dwarf spheroidal galaxies. This is a direct consequence of the association between UFDs and low-mass mini-halos. The detection of a single CEMP-no star in Pisces II do not allow us to make statistical comparisons with model predictions. However, our observational confirmation of one CEMP-no star out of the four stars identified by at [ Fe/H ] < -2 supports the idea that the probability of observing CEMP-no stars is high in ultra-faint dwarf galaxies. In Pisces II only seven stars have been spectroscopically observed KirbySC15 , and one of these stars , was found to be very metal-poor and was suspected to be C-rich.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Psc II as Peg III's nearby dwarf-satellite companion candidate, sharing similar distance and velocity and motivating tests of a possible bound pair or interaction.

quotes.quote: Peg III and Psc II are approximately 43 kpc away from each other in three dimensions (3D) and their radial velocities in the Galactic standard-of-rest (GSR) frame differ only by 10 km s ^ 1 ( v_ GSR =-67.6 2.6 km s ^ 1 for Peg III and v_ GSR =-79.9 2.7 km s ^ 1 for Psc II). Given that only relatively few distant MW satellite galaxies are presently known, the close spatial proximity of Peg III and Psc II, and their very similar radial velocities, suggest a possible association between them.

Assuming that the two satellites are currently a bound pair with an equal halo mass and follow a circular orbit on their average Galactocentric distance of 198 10 kpc, we have estimated their total halo mass following the method of . This method estimates the mass of the binary satellite system on the basis of the Virial theorem using the difference in their line-of-sight velocities ( 12.3 3.7 km s ^ 1 ) and the separation between its constituents ( d_ spatial =43 19 kpc) in 3D space. The derived mass of a satellite halo is 2.3 1.7 10^ 9 M_ , which yields a tidal radius of r_t=16 4 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper uses Kirby probabilities for Pisces II, keeps only two stars, finds no supporting photometric candidates, and judges the systemic proper motion comparatively unreliable.

quotes.quote: Pisces II We use the catalog of Kirby_15 , which provides binary probabilities. Only two stars (9004 and 12924 in the nomenclature of table 2 of Kirby_15 ) pass our cuts and there are no additional stars w/o spectroscopy that we can use to back up our determination. This is partly expected due to the faintness of the galaxy. Clearly, the systemic PM is less reliable than most of our determination, but its error is anyway rather large.

gmw_00008674.name: G1/G2
gmw_00008674.overview: G1/G2 is a close projected pair of galaxies located near a central quasar [arXiv:1811.10615]. The system exhibits a 60 kiloparsec arm of continuum emission to the north, which suggests ongoing tidal interactions between the quasar host and G1/G2 [arXiv:1811.10615].

sources.summary: G1/G2 are described as close projected galaxies near the quasar, plausibly interacting with its host and connected to continuum and tidal-arm-like nebular structures.

quotes.quote: G1/G2 are close projected pairs with one another ( 10 pkpc) and with the quasar (14 and 22 pkpc respectively). The quasar light subtracted MUSE image shown in the top left panel of Figure figure:narrows reveals an arm of continuum emission extending 60 pkpc to the North of the quasar, a signature of recent or on-going interactions, possibly between the quasar host and G1/G2.
H.N. and H.S. and the extended arm of continuum emission seen North of the host may be the result of on-going interactions with G1/G2 or another more advanced merger.

gmw_00008675.name: G6/G7
gmw_00008675.overview: G6/G7 is an interacting pair of galaxies with a projected separation of 34 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1811.10615]. Both galaxies in the pair exhibit red rest-frame colors and are enveloped by a morphologically and kinematically coincident ionized nebula [arXiv:1811.10615].

sources.summary: G6/G7 is treated as an interacting red galaxy pair in the quasar group; its associated ionized nebula is interpreted as stripped ISM from ongoing interactions.

quotes.quote: The galaxy morphologies, projected separations, and relative velocities indicate that G6/G7, G9/G11, and G8/G10 are interacting galaxy pairs with projected separations of 34 , 9 , and 7 pkpc respectively. G9/G11 are also nearly spatially coincident with one of the quasar radio lobes see Sambruna:2004 which is labelled with a blue triangle in Figure figure:narrows . All six of the interacting galaxies exhibit red rest-frame colors of u g = 1.1 to 1.7 .

The three largest and most luminous nebulae (S., E.S.E., and E.) are kinematically and morphologically coincident with interacting galaxies in the field as shown in Figure figure:narrows . In particular, S., E.S.E, and E. spatially and kinematically envelope interacting galaxy pairs G9/G11, G8/G10, and G6/G7 respectively. The morphological and kinematic correspondence with interacting galaxies and the narrow internal velocity dispersions ( = 70 110 ) are most consistent with stripped ISM resulting from on-going interactions despite the spatial coincidence between the S. nebulae and a radio lobe .f. Harrison:2015 .

gmw_00008676.name: G9/G11
gmw_00008676.overview: G9/G11 is an interacting pair of red galaxies with a projected separation of 9 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1811.10615]. The pair is spatially and kinematically enveloped by an associated ionized nebula, which likely consists of interstellar medium stripped during their ongoing interaction [arXiv:1811.10615].

sources.summary: G9/G11 is treated as an interacting red galaxy pair in the quasar group; its associated ionized nebula is interpreted as stripped ISM from ongoing interactions.

quotes.quote: The galaxy morphologies, projected separations, and relative velocities indicate that G6/G7, G9/G11, and G8/G10 are interacting galaxy pairs with projected separations of 34 , 9 , and 7 pkpc respectively. The morphological and kinematic correspondence with interacting galaxies and the narrow internal velocity dispersions ( = 70 110 ) are most consistent with stripped ISM resulting from on-going interactions despite the spatial coincidence between the S. nebulae and a radio lobe .f. Harrison:2015 .

gmw_00008677.name: G8/G10
gmw_00008677.overview: G8/G10 is an interacting pair of red galaxies with a projected separation of 7 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1811.10615]. The pair is spatially and kinematically enveloped by an associated ionized nebula, which likely consists of interstellar medium stripped during their ongoing interaction [arXiv:1811.10615].
